experiments.title: How does educational language about tribute affect tribute donations on Single Post Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding education language on the SPP tribute widget for SPP visitors will increase donations by reducing friction around what the tribute gift is, providing clarity about what it can do, and encouraging users to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2026 - 03/16/2026
CaringBridge previously has had success with having language that is educational about what tribute giving is and how the readers tribute gift makes a difference.
For this test, we wanted to test adding that language on their Single Post Page tribute widget to see how that would affect tribute donations on the . This was tested for both mobile and desktop visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding educational language to the tribute widget significantly boosted all key metrics.
Donations (Tribute/JEN): +108.3% (99.9% confidence)
 	Transactions rose by 489%. Revenue overall increased by 604%. Donations made as tributes went up by 779%. Tribute revenue jumped by 886%. Tribute revenue for JEN specifically increased by 880%. These shifts demonstrate that educational language led to more transactions, more tribute giving, and much higher associated revenue.
In short, clearer information and less friction increase donations.
Future experiments should explore:
Testing educational language in other areas of the website or in different formats
 	Expanding this language across additional tribute widgets to understand its effect in different contexts

experiments.title: How adding a impact statement below the donate button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact statement below the donate button for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting the potential impact of their gift at the donate button would act as an extra layer of reinforcement and encourage the user to donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2025
CaringBridge had a 2 day match. For this match, we wanted to test adding an impact statement below the donate button that highlighted that their gift would be doubled during this match. The statement read as follows –
Your $x gift goes twice as far, keeping this a free platform for patients, caregivers, and families to receive support and coordinate care every single day throughout a difficult health journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the impact statement below the donate button did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant 21.5% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.7% confidence level.

In future experimentation, it's important to carefully consider the messaging and placement of impact statements or any additional content on donation pages. It's possible that the impact statement used in this experiment was not compelling enough or may have caused confusion or distraction for potential donors.

There was a decrease in revenue as well. There was an 18% decrease in revenue with a 41% level of confidence

Control - $17,443

Treatment - $14,520

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to test different variations of impact statements, adjust the placement of the statement on the page, or even consider other strategies to emphasize the impact of donations. Additionally, gathering feedback from donors through surveys or qualitative research could provide valuable insights into what resonates with them and what motivates them to donate.

Overall, while this experiment did not yield the desired results, it provides valuable insights that can inform and improve future fundraising strategies for CaringBridge.

experiments.title: Does an educational slideout, with no ask, increase tribute giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that educational copy with no ask for users on a site profile will increase donor conversion rates because it provides context for the ask and greater clarity around tribute giving.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/03/2026
CaringBridge’s platform is driven largely by tribute donations, gifts that are made to CaringBridge on a user’s page in honor of them, often with an accompanying message to the author. However, we hypothesized that users may not fully understand how tribute giving works: with questions about where their gift goes or why it’s impactful to an author’s page. We ran an A/B/C test in which both treatments contained no additional ask—but simply provided educational copy and context—believing this would better support the existing donation widgets.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that providing educational copy with no direct ask on users' profiles significantly increased donation conversions by 63.8%. This indicates that users are more likely to donate when they better understand the purpose and impact of tribute donations on CaringBridge. In future experiments, we should continue testing the effectiveness of educational copy and messaging in driving donation conversions. We can further refine the messaging to see if specific language or framing yields even higher conversion rates, and whether there's a way to optimize the experience so the slideout isn't intrusive in the long term.

experiments.title: How an exit intent vs a slideup affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing a slide up vs an exit intent for site profiles will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the slide up reduced the friction of a user having to select the gift amount on the donation page, and the exit intent encouraged the user to help CB meet the match as others have done.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
For CaringBridge, we had previously tested two slide up versions and three exit-intent versions.
For this test, we wanted to compare the winning versions of the slide up and exit intent to see how they would perform.
This was tested on both mobile and desktop and on site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From the results of this experiment, we can see that the slide up had a 16.1% increase in transactions, with a 96.8% level of confidence. Therefore, this test is valid.
What's interesting here is that, when looking at the results of the experiment by desktop and mobile traffic, there was a slight decrease in transactions for the slideup on desktop traffic, but a significant increase in donations on mobile traffic.
This helps determine that the slide up is a more optimized experience for mobile, and there could be opportunities to optimize the exit intent for mobile users to help mobile traffic respond better.

experiments.title: How a Yes/No Slideout vs a Slideout with a gift array affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a slideout with a gift array for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in transactions because the slideout with the gift array would reduce friction for a user while making a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2025 - 12/08/2025
For CaringBridge, we have previously seen that a slideout with a yes/no options has been successful during a year-end campaign. For this slideout, we wanted to test that slideout vs a slideout with a gift array. This was tested on the homepage of CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The slideout with a gift array on the homepage of CaringBridge resulted in a significant increase in donations for all traffic, with a 73.6% increase at a 98.7% level of confidence.
2. In particular, the One-Time Donation option saw a remarkable 672% increase with a 98% level of confidence.
3. The hypothesis that the slideout with a gift array would reduce friction for users while making a gift proved to be true.
Moving forward, we should consider implementing the slideout with a gift array on other key pages of the CaringBridge website to optimize donation opportunities. Additionally, we could explore further testing variations of gift arrays to determine which types are most effective in driving donations. It is crucial to continue monitoring and analyzing user behavior to enhance the overall donation experience on the platform.

experiments.title: How adding a Quick Comment box to posts affects comments on a post and overall tribute donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a quick comment box for single post page visitors and profile visitors will increase comments because having that box would reduce the friction of having to think about what to comment on and encourage them to leave a positive comment on a post.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2025 - 09/12/2025
CaringBridge wanted to find ways to increase comments on posts. One test we wanted to do was to have a quick comment box on the first post on the profile feed and on single post pages. This box had some prompts where someone could click on any of the prompts, and that would populate in the comment.
This was tested on both mobile and desktop. We wanted to test how having an easy way to comment with some suggested prompts would affect comments.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a quick comment box with suggested prompts for single post page visitors and profile visitors led to a 5.8% increase in comments left for all traffic, with a high level of statistical confidence. This supports the hypothesis that reducing users' friction by providing prompts can encourage them to leave positive comments.

2. While there was a valid lift in comments, there was a decrease in tribute donations overall and on the single post page.

Overall Tribute Donations - 

Control : 311,312 samples | 633 Donations

Treatment : 309,625 | 565 Donations

10.3% Decrease with 93.9% level of confidence

Tribute Donations on Single Post Pages - 

Control : 311,312 samples | 178 Donations

Treatment : 309,625 samples | 158 Donations

10.8% decrease with 70% level of confidence

Future experimentation should build on these learnings by:

1. Continuing to explore the impact of reducing user friction in different platform aspects to increase engagement and interactions.

2. Conducting further research to understand the unexpected impact on donations and how it can be mitigated or leveraged in future experiments.

3.Considering additional factors that may influence user behavior when implementing new features or changes to the platform, it is essential to anticipate and address any potential unintended consequences.

experiments.title: How adding an animated heart on a tribute widget affects tribute revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an animated heart to tribute widget CTA’s for profile visitors will achieve an increase in revenue because adding the animated heart would capture the attention of the user and stand out, bringing more clarity to what the widget is and what the ask is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2025 - 08/01/2025
For CaringBridge, we wanted to test adding an animated heart to the CTA of tribute widgets to see how that would affect tribute donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an animated heart to the CTA of tribute widgets for profile visitors resulted in a significant 13.0% increase in Revenue - Tribute for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This means that the treatment was successful in capturing the attention of users and driving more donations.

In future experimentation, we should consider incorporating more visual elements or animations into our CTAs to make them more engaging and attention-grabbing. It's important to continue testing different design elements and messaging to see what resonates best with our audience and drives the most donations. experiments.title: How an exit intent on single post pages affects comments
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent popup for single post page visitors will achieve an increase in comments on a post because the copy in the exit intent encouraged the user to leave a comment, highlighting the importance of leaving a comment for the author.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2025 - 08/01/2025
For CaringBridge, you can go to a page that has a single post on it if you want to go to a specific post. In an effort to encourage users to leave comments on posts, we wanted to test having an exit intent popup with copy that would encourage a user to leave a comment for the author on their post. This experiment targeted desktop users. The exit intent was shown on pages with posts where a user hadn’t commented on the post.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:
1. The addition of an exit intent popup for single post page visitors led to a significant increase in comments on a post by 9.6% with a high level of statistical confidence (99.9%). This indicates that the copy in the exit intent, which encouraged users to leave a comment for the author, was effective in driving user engagement.
2. Revenue - Tribute (JEN) also experienced a substantial increase of 17.2% with a 100% level of confidence. This shows that not only did the exit intent popup result in more user engagement in the form of comments, but it also positively impacted revenue generation for single post page tribute widget.
3. The results suggest that prompting users to leave comments through an exit intent popup can be a successful strategy for increasing user engagement and potentially driving revenue for Caringbridge.
We also saw a positive increase in tribute donations, which would indicate that the presence of the exit intent did not discourage users to make a tribute gift, but rather encouraged users to make a gift.
Future Experimentation Recommendations:
1. Implement similar exit intent popups on other pages of the website to test if this strategy can be scaled across different sections for further increasing user engagement and revenue.
2. Test different variations of the exit intent copy to optimize the messaging and further enhance its effectiveness in encouraging users to leave comments and contribute towards the nonprofit's objectives.
3. Explore the possibility of personalizing the exit intent popups based on user behavior or demographics to make the messaging more relevant and compelling to different audience segments.
4. Consider conducting qualitative research, such as user feedback surveys or interviews, to gain deeper insights into user preferences and attitudes towards the exit intent popups, helping to refine the strategy for future implementations.

experiments.title: How does donor-centric language on a match sticky bar impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donor-centric language on the match sticky bar for users on the CaringBridge site will achieve an increase in conversion rate because priorities the donor as the hero and highlights the impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2025
Frequently throughout the year, CaringBridge will have short, 2-day match campaigns. These sprints are driven by urgency and the opportunity for donors to have their gift doubled—dollar for dollar. During these campaigns, CaringBridge has specific language they use regarding the match, but we hypothesized that it paints the provider of the match, rather than the donor themselves, as the hero. We believe that by adjusting the language, making it centered around the donor and their impact—versus leading with who has provided the match and for how much—will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that donor-centric language on the match sticky bar led to a significant increase in donations on the CaringBridge site. By prioritizing the donor as the hero and highlighting the impact of their gift, the conversion rate increased by 40.5%. This indicates that donors respond positively to messaging that focuses on their role and the significance of their contribution.

Perhaps the most impactful part of this experiment is that it's a learning CaringBridge can now begin to leverage every time they have a match sticky bar running. In addition to the increase in donor conversion rate, it also worth documenting the increase in clicks and revenue as well.

The treatment saw a 16% increase in clicks with a valid level of confidence, alongside a 33.2% increase in evenue with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a highlighted comment box for visitors on an author profile will achieve an increase comments because it increases the visibility of the prompt and section.
experiments.summary:

On CaringBridge user profiles, visitors are prompted to leave a comment with a supportive data point. We hypothesized that by increasing the visibility of this section, with a blue highlight over the comment box, will increase the number of comments.

The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the comment box on user profiles led to a 3.6% increase in comments left by visitors. This result was statistically significant at a confidence level of 97.6%. This indicates that increasing the visibility of the comment prompt can positively impact user engagement and interaction on the platform.

In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to enhance the visibility of interactive features on the platform. This could include experimenting with different colors, placement, or wording of prompts to further optimize user engagement. Additionally, it would be valuable to test the impact of highlighted comment boxes on other metrics such as return visits or user satisfaction to fully understand the overall impact on user experience. By consistently testing and analyzing different strategies for improving user interaction, CaringBridge can continue to enhance its platform and better support its users during challenging times.

experiments.title: How removing the site actions menu on single post pages affects tribute donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the site actions menu for single post page visitors will achieve an increase in donations to the tribute widget because removing that menu with conflicting cta would increase clarity on that page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 05/06/2025
CaringBridge has the ability for a visitor to view a single post on a page. On this page, there is a site actions menu, which has different call to action (cta) such as follow the page, share the page, etc. For this experiment, we wanted to test how removing that menu would affect donations to the tribute widget on the single post pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Removing the site actions menu for single post page visitors significantly increased donations to the tribute widget by 40.7% with a 96.0% level of confidence. This aligns with the hypothesis that removing conflicting cta can improve clarity and drive desired actions.
 	The Site Actions - Ways To Help clicked JEN experienced a dramatic decrease of 964% with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that the presence of the site actions menu may have been distracting or deterring visitors from making donations.
Simplify the user experience: Removing unnecessary cta or menu options can help streamline the user experience and direct visitors towards the desired action.
 	Focus on clarity and reducing distractions: By minimizing conflicting cta or extraneous elements on the page, we can increase clarity and guide visitors towards taking the desired actions.
 	Continue testing and iterating: It's important to continue testing different elements on the page to optimize donation conversions. By utilizing A/B testing and analyzing the results, we can further refine our approach and improve outcomes for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How reordering the Tribute and Requests tab affect tribute revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reordering the Tribute and Requests tab for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute revenue because reordering the tabs so that the tribute widget is on the end would give more prominence to that tab and draw more attention to it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2025 - 06/11/2025
For this CaringBridge experiment, we wanted to test whether reordering the Requests and Tributes tab would affect the tribute revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Reordering the Requests and Tributes tab to prioritize the Tribute tab resulted in a 17.3% increase in revenue from tribute donations. This indicates that giving more prominence to the Tribute tab can lead to a significant boost in revenue.
 	Despite the increase in revenue, there was a 91% decrease in Tribute Tab clicks. This suggests that while fewer people clicked on the Tribute Tab, those who did were more likely to make a donation.
 	We should consider the overall revenue impact rather than just focusing on click-through rates when analyzing the effectiveness of tab placement.
 	Future experimentation could involve testing different placements of the Tribute tab to optimize for both click-through rates and revenue generation. It is also important to continue monitoring user behavior and donation patterns to make data-driven decisions for improving the user experience and maximizing donations on the platform.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout with an animated heart affects donations on tribute page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly callout with an animated heart for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the callout has reinforcement language that would reaffirm the reason they would donate. because  .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 05/13/2025
For this experiment, we wanted to add a monthly callout with an animated heart to see how it would affect donations on the tribute donation page. The way this experiment works is when a user lands on the donation page, they would see an animated heart on the monthly tab, and when they would click on the monthly tab, a callout would pop up with reinforcement language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
1. Adding a monthly callout with an animated heart to the tribute donation page led to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group, which experienced the callout, saw a 13.9% increase in transactions compared to the control group.
2. The statistical significance of 97.2% gives us confidence that the results are reliable and not due to random chance.
3. The hypothesis that the callout with reinforcement language would reaffirm the reason for donating proved to be correct.
4. The use of animation, visual elements, and callout language can positively impact donation behavior.
It is important to note here that although there weren't many recurring donations, there was a valid increase in transactions.
How to Use Learnings in Future Experimentation:
1. Consider incorporating more visual and interactive elements into donation pages to capture and maintain donors' attention.
2. Continue to test different types of callout language and visual cues to see which ones resonate most with donors.
3. Conduct further experiments to understand the specific elements within the callout that were most effective in driving donations.
4. Apply these learnings to other areas of the website or marketing materials to see if similar tactics can be successful in different contexts.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of creative and strategic elements in donation pages to motivate and inspire donors to contribute to the cause.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky navigation bar on mobile affects clicks on Donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a stacked navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in clicks to the donate button because adding that stacked bar would increase clarity among the buttons and look cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2025 - 05/20/2025
For CaringBridge, their mobile navigation bar consisted of multiple buttons where there was minimal space and all the buttons looked very small and stuck together. For this test, we wanted to change the layout of the navigation bar. We added a space above the original navigation bar and added the donate and search button on the extra space above the nav bar. The stacked part with the search button and donate button was sticky, so users could see it while scrolling through. We wanted to test if doing this would affect clicks to the donate button for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

 	Adding a stacked navigation bar for mobile users led to a significant increase in clicks to the donate button (72.4% increase). This indicates that making the donate button more prominent and easily accessible can positively impact user engagement with this feature.
 	The navigation search button also saw a substantial increase in clicks (676% increase). This suggests that improving the visibility and usability of buttons in the navigation bar can drive higher interaction rates across multiple features.

In future experimentation, we should consider the following:

 	Continue to prioritize the visibility and accessibility of key features, such as the donate button, by strategically placing them in the navigation bar where they are easily noticeable and clickable.
 	Use A/B testing and other experimentation methods to iteratively optimize the layout and design of the mobile navigation bar to enhance user experience and drive engagement with important features.
 	Consider applying similar strategies to other areas of the website or app to improve overall user interaction and achieve organizational goals, such as increasing donations or promoting specific actions.

experiments.title: How relocating the tribute widget on single post pages affects tribute donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that relocating the tribute widget to below the post for single post page visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donations because the tribute widget wouldn’t be perceived as a paywall and the user would be able to focus on the messaging of the widget rather than see it as a distraction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2025 - 05/12/2025
On CaringBridge’s single post pages, the tribute widget is located above the post. For a regular user, it may be perceived as a paywall to have to read or interact with the post, and therefore may be off putting for the user. For this experiment, we wanted to see how moving the tribute widget to be below the post would affect donations to the tribute widget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that relocating the tribute widget to below the post on single post pages led to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group experienced a 110.8% increase in Donations - Tribute (JEN) for All Traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence, indicating that the change was highly impactful.

Furthermore, there was a substantial 426% increase in Donations - Tribute overall, with a 96% level of confidence. This indicates that not only did moving the widget below the post lead to more donations specifically to the JEN tribute, but it also had a positive impact on overall tribute donations.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the placement of donation widgets and ensure that they are not perceived as barriers to accessing content. This experiment shows that small changes in design and placement can have a significant impact on user behavior and donation outcomes. It is essential to continue testing different placements and designs to optimize donation widgets for maximum impact and user engagement.

CaringBridge has a menu for their Site Actions for Mobile Users. For this test, we wanted to test a new layout for the menu, where there was a fixed layout at the bottom of the screen, which had all button listed. This test was done for authors on mobile.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a fixed toolbar layout for mobile authors significantly increased the clicks on the menu by 131.0% with a statistical level of confidence of 100.0%. This indicates that the fixed layout at the bottom of the screen reduced friction and made it easier for mobile authors to access the menu.

Along with the number of clicks to any button, we can see that even for individual metrics, there are increases for the treatment.

In future experimentation, we should consider implementing fixed toolbar layouts for other user segments or testing different variations of the fixed toolbar layout to further optimize the user experience. Additionally, we should continue to prioritize reducing friction and improving accessibility in all design decisions based on the success of this experiment.

experiments.title: How adding a stacked navigation bar affects clicks on the search bar
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that stacking the search button and Donate button for mobile users will achieve an increase in clicks on the buttons because having those stacked would make them more clear and reduce friction in clicking the buttons.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2025 - 04/28/2025
For mobile users, the CaringBridge navigation bar had all buttons on one line, all next to each other. For this test, we wanted to test having the search bar and Donate button stacked on top of the original navigation bar to see how that would affect clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Stacking the search button and Donate button for mobile users resulted in a significant increase in clicks on the Navigation Search Button for All Traffic by 39.8% with a 100.0% level of confidence. This suggests that the treatment was successful in improving user engagement with the search functionality.
2. However, the experiment also showed that Donations - Non-Tribute experienced a 1000% decrease with a 97% level of confidence, and Navigation Donate Button clicked experienced a 430% decrease with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that the stacked layout may have had a negative impact on donation-related interactions.
3. Moving forward, it is important to consider the trade-offs between improving certain functionalities (such as search) and potentially hindering others (such as donation clicks). It may be necessary to find a balance between these factors in future experiments to ensure overall positive outcomes.
4. It is also important to continue monitoring and analyzing the data to understand the impact of design changes on user behavior accurately. A/B testing provides valuable insights that can inform future decision-making and optimization strategies.
In future experimentation, it is essential to carefully consider the potential impacts of design changes on different user actions and prioritize based on the organization's goals. Testing variations and analyzing the results will help in making informed decisions to optimize the user experience effectively.

experiments.title: How a hyper-short popup affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a hyper-short popup for mobile users will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because since these are mobile users, a short popup would create less friction as there was less to look through, therefore driving the click.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
CaringBridge has a popup on their website that has encourages the user to make a recurring gift. For this test, we wanted to test a hyper-short treatment because we were testing the mobile version of the popup. This popup appeared every 4th visit.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the hyper-short popup for mobile users did not lead to an increase in click-through rate. In fact, it resulted in a significant decrease in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that the hypothesis was not supported by the data, and in this case, a shorter popup may have actually deterred users from clicking.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of the changes being implemented. It is possible that in some cases, providing more information or content in the popup may actually be more effective in engaging users and prompting them to take action. Additionally, it is crucial to always test hypotheses with a control group to accurately measure the impact of the changes.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of data-driven decision-making and the need to carefully evaluate the potential outcomes of any changes before implementation.

experiments.title: How changing the site actions menu for mobile users affects clicks

rows.name: Fixed Toolbar Layout Site Actions Menu

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in these two different site action menus for authors will achieve an increase in clicks on site actions because these two variants gave a more clear look of the buttons.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2025 - 04/01/2025
For CaringBridge, we wanted to test different variants of the site actions menu for mobile user. The first variant has all the buttons stacked at the top of the page, and stays at the top of the page. The second variant has a fixed toolbar on the bottom of the page with all the buttons.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment group, which had the fixed toolbar at the bottom of the page, experienced a significant decrease in clicks on the "Site Actions - Create Post" button compared to the control group with the stacked buttons at the top of the page. This indicates that the bottom toolbar may not be as effective for encouraging users to create a new post.

2. However, the treatment group did see a substantial increase in clicks on the "Any new menu" button. This suggests that the fixed toolbar may be more prominent and eye-catching for users, leading to increased engagement with this particular button.

3. The treatment group also experienced significant decreases in clicks for other site actions such as "Add A Co-Author", "Share Page", "Ways To Help", and "Page Settings". This indicates that the fixed toolbar may not be as intuitive or user-friendly for accessing these features.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the placement and design of site action menus to optimize user engagement. This experiment highlights the importance of balancing visibility and usability when designing interface elements. It may be beneficial to conduct further research and testing to identify the most effective layout for site action menus that encourages user interaction and engagement across a variety of actions.

experiments.title: How adding in education copy in the widget affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in educational copy in the tribute widget for JEN traffic will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because that copy would give more clarity as to what the ask is and encourage users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/19/2025 - 02/26/2025
For CaringBridge, we had previously ran the education copy in the tribute widget in the tributes tab. For this experiment, we wanted to how adding in that same copy in the tribute widget for JEN traffic would affect donor conversion rate.

This indicates that providing more clarity and information about the donation ask can encourage users to donate.

In future experimentation, we should consider integrating educational copy or information about the donation ask in various elements of our platform to potentially increase donor conversion rates. It is important to continue testing and iterating on different messaging strategies to optimize conversion rates and ultimately drive more donations for CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How does a closed menu and floating action button impact flywheel metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky, floating action button for authors that allow them to open their flywheel menu at anytime will achieve an increase in these “manage my page” actions because reduces the space and friction of the current menu, while also prioritizing these flywheel options in a way that remains on the page easily.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2025 - 02/24/2025
A key metric for CaringBridge is the Author flywheel. These are a list of menu items labelled “Manage My Page” with critical actions like sharing their page, making a post, starting a Ways to Get Help, and more. In previous testing, we’ve found that all of these metrics are improved by simply having this menu open when the author arrive on their page in mobile. However, CaringBridge feared this extra step of having to close it would be friction for the user. As an alternative, our teams developed two iterations of a Floating Action Button that stayed sticky on the screen and would open this action menu.
The control, kept the current menu open as is.
Treatment 1 closed the menu, but added the floating action button.
Treatment 2 completely removed the menu, and adding a floating action button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The control, which kept the current menu open, outperformed the treatments that introduced a floating action button to open the menu. This indicates that the existing menu layout may be more effective in prompting authors to take actions on their page.

2. Ultimately, the FAB did not translate into increased actions such as sharing the page, creating a post, or accessing page settings. In fact, some actions experienced a significant decrease in clicks with the FAB.

4. It is crucial to consider the impact of design changes on specific actions and metrics. While the FAB may attract attention and clicks, it may not always lead to the desired outcomes.

In future experimentation, it would be valuable to further investigate the reasons behind the decrease in key actions with the FAB implementation. Understanding why users may not be taking certain actions with the FAB could provide insights into the effectiveness of this design element and how it can be optimized.

experiments.title: How does an additional Share to Socials prompt impact share clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a Share to Socials prompt for authors after they’ve just made a post will achieve an increase in Share Clicks because it presents this option in an intentional way, directly after an action that is related and relevant.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2025 - 03/03/2025
Currently, CaringBridge authors have a “Share” prompt in their flywheel/manage my page options. We hypothesized that presenting this pop-up prompt at the end of another relevant experience, creating post, would increase overall Share Clicks. Posting to their feed is an action that can then relevantly followed up with a share to socials—improving overall metrics by introducing another way to make this action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Timing is crucial: Presenting prompts or options to users immediately after they have completed a related action can significantly increase engagement. In this case, asking authors to share their post after they have just made one led to a significant increase in Share Clicks.

2. Relevance is key: Making the prompt or option relevant to the user's current experience can make them more likely to engage. By offering a "Share to Socials" prompt after a user has just made a post, the prompt felt intentional and related, resulting in a higher engagement rate.

3. Testing and iteration are essential: Running A/B experiments can help determine the effectiveness of new features or prompts. By continuously testing and iterating based on the results, we can optimize user engagement and overall metrics.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the impact of presenting prompts or options at strategic times, such as immediately after a related action. Additionally, we should always consider the relevance of the prompt to the user's current experience to increase engagement. By following these key learnings, we can continue to improve user interactions and drive desired outcomes for Caringbridge.

experiments.title: How underlining post titles increases clicks to single-post page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that underlining post titles for users on an author profile will achieve an increase in clicks to the single-post page and by extension Tribute Gifts because it directs more traffic to a widget that, otherwise, users are not aware how to get to.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2025 - 02/10/2025
We hypothesized that in the current experience, users are not aware that they can click the titles of an Author’s Post—leading them to the single-post page view. To test this hypothesis, we wanted to observe the impact of underlining this title in the treatment, more clearly denoting that it was clickable. This would have two potential impacts. First, it could help improve the user experience by allowing them to read longer posts in a designated window versus in-feed. Second, it would increase traffic to the single-page view. On this page, they are presented with a Tribute Widget (16003). We hypothesized that directing more traffic to this widget would also increase donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that underlining post titles on an author profile significantly increased the number of clicks to the single-post page. This successfully led to a substantial increase in traffic to the single-post page. Confirming that in the current experience, users are not aware that the titles can be clicked. However, this did not by extension lead to an increase in Tribute widget gifts as hypothesized.

When considering the user journey, these results can be understood better. While conversion rate on this widget is typically high for JEN traffic (those clicking from an email and going to the post) it may not convert as highly for traffic who is coming to the single-post page from the user's profile. In the latter, they are already shown tribute widgets and asks on the author's profile, seeing another on the single-post page doesn't necessarily inspire them to give on its own. Unlike JEN traffic, which is clicking from an email where no ask is present and arriving on a page with an ask.

However, what these results underscore is the potential importance of increasing traffic while also optimizing the widget. If more users arrived on this page AND were presented with a unique ask, it could potentially convert that traffic into donors.

experiments.title: How a tooltip on Ways to Help impacts clicks and Gift Card setups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tooltip describing what step one should take for authors will achieve an increase in Gift Card setups because adding that tooltip will give clarity on what the user should do and help reduce confusion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2025 - 01/31/2025
CaringBridge has the option for authors to set up a gift card so that people visiting their page can send the author a gift card as a way to help. For this test, we tried adding a tooltip that focused on what the user should do and specifying what the option is used for.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a tooltip describing what step the user should take for setting up a gift card for authors led to a significant increase in the Save button clicked for gift card setups on the website. The treatment group experienced a 363.9% increase in Save button clicked for gift cards on the What They Heard page for all traffic, with a statistical significance level of 100.0%.
Furthermore, the Tooltip CTA button clicked also saw a significant increase of 1,383.8% with 100% statistical confidence. This indicates that the tooltip providing clarity on what the user should do helped reduce confusion and encourage more users to set up gift cards.
In future experiments, it is important to continue emphasizing the importance of providing clear instructions and guidance for users to navigate the website effectively. This includes using tooltips or other visual cues to assist users in understanding the steps they need to take. Additionally, the success of this experiment highlights the value of testing different strategies for improving user experience and conversion rates. By continuing to experiment and iterate on website design and functionalities, we can further optimize the user experience and achieve better results.

experiments.title: How an identity call to action on an exit intent impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an identity CTA “Yes, I can be one of the 3 donors” for users encountering the exit-intent during a 2-day match will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it empowers the donor with autonomy to answer the question through the CTA “Yes” and emphasizing identity rather than action “I can be one of the donors”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we tested a variety of identity CTA’s throughout the campaign. In this particular experiment, we tested a CTA on the 2-day match exit intent “Yes, I can be one of the 3 donors.” On the Tribute Widget, we saw a similar autonomy CTA emphasizing that the reader was willing to give $25 significantly improved the conversion rate. In this iteration, the language focused more on donor identity ” I can be one of the donors” rather than focusing on a giving amount. We believe this autonomy and donor identity treatment will increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the identity CTA "Yes, I can be one of the 3 donors" for users encountering the exit-intent during a 2-day match did not achieve an increase in donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment actually saw a 39.0% decrease in donations for all traffic with a high level of statistical significance.

This outcome suggests that in this particular context, emphasizing donor identity and autonomy through the CTA was not as effective in converting donors compared to other approaches. Throughout the holiday appeal, autonomy and identity language required specific parameters in order to be effective; working well in some instances but decreasing results in others. In the future, it would be worthwhile to determine the variable and discover the commonality between when autonomy succeeds and when it detracts from CaringBridge's audience.

Overall, it is important to take away from this experiment that not all hypotheses will yield positive results, and it is essential to continually test and refine strategies based on data and insights gathered from experiments.

experiments.title: How rotating a Ways to Help card in the Tribute Widget Impacts Clicks and Tribute Gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rotating Tribute Widget that displays a Ways to Help card 50% of the time for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in Send a Gift card traffic while having minimal impact on the tribute widget because the variety of calls to action will encourage users to explore other ways to give while also not presenting them with the same Tribute ask 100% of the time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2024 - 12/02/2024
On CaringBrige’s Tribute Widget, we hypothesized that having this card alternate (showing a Ways to Help gift card opportunity) 50% of the time would result in an increase of Send a Gift card traffic, while also having minimal impact on the Tribute Gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

1. **Increased Engagement:** The variation that displayed the Ways to Help gift card opportunity 50% of the time resulted in a significant increase in Send a Gift Card clicked for All Traffic by 41.8%. This indicates that offering a variety of calls to action can encourage users to explore different ways of giving.

2. **Positive Impact on Ways to Help Clicks:** The experiment also led to a 153% increase in the Ways to Help button in the menu clicked, a 3,457% increase in the Ways to Help widget CTA button clicked, and a 418% increase in Send a Gift Card clicked (src=widget). This demonstrates that featuring different giving options can drive higher engagement.

3. **Decrease in Tribute Donations:** The experiment resulted in a 398% decrease in Donations - Tribute clicked. While this may seem like a negative outcome, it indicates that users were more inclined to explore other giving opportunities beyond the Tribute widget.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the impact of rotating calls to action on user engagement. By offering a variety of giving options and monitoring their performance, we can optimize the user experience and maximize the impact of our fundraising efforts. Additionally, we should explore ways to further personalize the calls to action based on user behavior and preferences to drive even higher engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a Testimonial Carousel Compares to a Slide-Up with Gift Array
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a testimony carousel for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because in comparison to the normal slideup, it helps incorporate and visualize the real impact a donor’s gift has on those who use the CaringBridge platform every day.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2024 - 12/14/2024
During CaringBridge’s high-urgency appeal, we hypothesized that a testimony carousel would prove more emotive and effective at converting donors than the standard slide-up. In the control, users were presented with a slide-up that contained three giving options. In the treatment, users were presented with a similar slide-up onsite, but besides the copy was a rotating carousel of real images and stories from the CaringBridge blog about who is impacted by the platform.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the testimony carousel, which was the treatment, did not achieve an increase in donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, it led to a significant decrease of 62.3% in Donations compared to the control.
This outcome suggests that the rotating carousel of real images and stories from the CaringBridge blog may have been less effective or possibly distracting for users, leading to a lower conversion rate. This may also indicate that the effectiveness of the control, is in using that right-hand side of the slideup in order to simplify the giving process rather than expanding on more reasons to give. Having a gift array that prefills on the donation page, is ultimately more impactful on the donor's completion of their gift than the additional testimonies on the carousel.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of conducting thorough testing and being open to unexpected outcomes in order to make informed decisions for future strategies and optimizations.

experiments.title: How adding a clickable button to a high-urgency sticky bar impacts donor conversions and clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a clickable button CTA for users on the CaringBridge site will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it makes the “next step” clear to the reader, rather than relying on the user to figure out that the underlined text is a hyperlink.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/13/2024
During CaringBridge’s high-urgency appeal, we hypothesized that adding a clickable button with a clear CTA to the donation page would improve clicks and downstream donor conversion as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a clickable button with a clear call-to-action (CTA) to the donation page resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which received the clickable button CTA, saw a 287.6% increase in donations compared to the control group. Furthermore, the results were statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the improvement was not due to chance.

In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize clear CTAs and user-friendly design elements to guide our audience towards taking the desired action. This experiment highlights the importance of making the "next step" clear to the reader, as it can have a substantial impact on donor conversion rates. Going forward, we should consider testing different variations of CTAs, buttons, and other design elements to continuously optimize the donation page and enhance the user experience.

experiments.title: How an affirming CTA on tribute widget impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an affirming CTA for users viewing a tribute widget will achieve an increase in donor conversino l because it empowers the reader with the autonomy to answer a question in the affirmative, reinforcing their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2024 - 12/04/2024
During CaringBridge’s 2-day match appeal, the Tribute widget copy would be updated to include mention of the match opportunity in addition to highlighting the usual $25 callout. In the control, the CTA button was “Donate to CaringBridge today.” We hypothesized that by turning this CTA into an affirmation of the question “Will you give $25?” would improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Affirming CTAs can positively impact donor conversion rates: The treatment, which featured an affirming CTA "Yes, I can give $25" resulted in a significant 32.5% increase in donations compared to the control. This indicates that empowering the reader with an affirmative question can reinforce their decision to give.
2. Statistical significance: The results showed a high level of statistical confidence at 98.9%, indicating that the observed increase in conversions is likely not due to random chance.
It's worth noting that this impact was seen during the match opportunity, however, it performed flat when run as an evergreen experiment. However, it's possible that the affirmative language benefits most from a sense of urgency.

experiments.title: How a Badge Icon paired with Tribute Widget Treatments Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that social proof and education copy on the Tribute Widget for users who are in the Tribute tab will achieve an increase in donor conversions and revenue because it will address the potential friction of not knowing what this widget is, who the gift is to, or why it has value to the profile.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2024 - 10/17/2024
On CaringBridge profiles, the Tribute widget is a significant source of donations and revenue. A tribute donation is a gift to CaringBridge that a user makes in tribute to the profile they are visiting. This widget appears in a few different locations on the site, but historically one of the highest-performing locations has been on the Tribute page itself. This tab shows a list of tributes that have been made on the user’s profile.
In a previous iteration, we are able to increase donations slightly by simply driving more traffic to this tab using an animated Badge Icon that showed the number of tribute widgets on the page. However, a large majority of what we saw was an increase in clicks that didn’t necessarily translate to more donations.
We hypothesized that by leveraging the increase in traffic brought on by the animated Badge while pairing it with a Social Proof and Education treatment copy on the widget itself would increase both clicks and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Incorporating social proof and education copy on the Tribute Widget can significantly increase donor conversions and revenue**: The treatment that included social proof and education copy on the Tribute Widget led to a substantial 204.4% increase in revenue from the widget. This indicates that providing users with more information about the widget and its value can positively impact their decision to donate.
Furthermore, the increase in revenue was statistically significant, with confidence levels of 98.0%. This reinforces the reliability of the results.
In future experimentation, we should continue to leverage the use of social proof and education copy on widgets to increase donor conversions and revenue. Additionally, we could explore other ways to drive traffic to key pages, such as using animated Badge Icons, to further enhance the effectiveness of our messaging. Conducting similar experiments on other high-traffic pages or widgets within the platform could also yield valuable insights and potentially increase overall donations. By continuously testing and optimizing our strategies based on learnings from experiments, we can continue to improve user engagement and drive more donations for CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs an exit-intent impacts donor conversion during high-urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that slide-up with gift array for users on profile sites during the 2-day match appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it allows the users to already begin the donation process, by selecting an amount, before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2024 - 09/26/2024
CaringBridge was running a 2-day high-urgency match appeal. Historically, this has been communicated using an exit-intent pop-up that informs users about how many donors are needed as well as that the match will double their impact. We hypothesized that a slide-up with a gift array of three amounts would ultimately be more eye-catching, empower the user with more options up front, and increase revenue by ensuring that whatever amount they selected would be pre-filled on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that utilizing a slide-up with a gift array during high-urgency match appeals can significantly increase donor conversion rates and revenue. In this particular case, the treatment resulted in a 45.5% increase in Donations - Non-Tribute for All Traffic with a high level of statistical confidence, indicating that the new approach was successful in prompting more users to donate.
Moving forward, these learnings should be applied to future experimentation by considering the effectiveness of providing users with donation options upfront, allowing them to start the donation process before reaching the actual donation page. This approach not only streamlines the donation process for users but also serves as a powerful motivator for them to contribute, particularly during time-sensitive campaigns like match appeals.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of user experience and the potential impact of small changes in the donation journey. It is essential to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to optimize fundraising efforts and ultimately drive more support for the organization's mission.
In the future, we would like to re-test this experience during an evergreen phase, outside of the 2-day match appeal to see its impact on donors and revenue.

experiments.title: How a slide out with educational copy increased site follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an educational slide out for visitors on an author page will achieve an increase in site follows because it not only draws more attention, but includes copy that addresses the value of following.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2024 - 08/28/2024
To improve site follows, CaringBridge had tested into a Sticky Follow button that kept this CTA pinned to the top of the screen if a user scrolled down. While this resulted in increased follows due to the greater visibility, we hypothesized that a second iteration that called attention to this CTA while also including educational copy around “why” a user should follow would increase this metric even more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing an educational slide out on the author page led to a significant increase in site follows. The treatment resulted in a 151.1% increase in Site Follows for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating a strong impact on user behavior.

What this indicates is that although increasing visibility was effective, the real question that needed to be addressed for users was why they should follow in the first place. By including copy that emphasizes the follow as a way to stay updated and receive important updates on the user's health journey, more users were likely to follow.

experiments.title: How pulling the Follow button out of a hidden dropdown in mobile impacts site follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pulling the Follow button out of the hidden dropdown menu for users in mobile will achieve an increase in Site Follows because it makes the call-to-action more visible, eliminating the potential friction of having to search for it while also highlighting its importance .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 08/20/2024
When visiting a CaringBridge user’s profile, key calls to action for the visitor are “Follow,” “Share,” and “Ways to Help.” However, in the mobile view we realized that all off these calls to action are hidden inside of a dropdown. Only after the user clicks “Show your support for [Name]” does it then reveal these prompts to follow their page, share it, or view their Ways to Help page.
We hypothesized that this was causing unnecessary friction and likely resulted in much of the audience not knowing that these actions are even present. We believe that by having this dropdown open by default in the treatment so that a user receives all of these options for the profile page, that it will increase Site Follows along with interaction to the other buttons. Having this dropdown open to reveal the Follow, Share, and Ways to Help in mobile was the only change in the treatment. The user also still had the option to minimize this dropdown and close out the options if preferred.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that making the Follow button more visible in the mobile view led to a significant increase in Site Follows, Dropdown Share button clicks, Dropdown Follow button clicks, and Dropdown Ways to Help button clicks. By pulling the Follow button out of the hidden dropdown menu and displaying it prominently, we were able to eliminate potential friction for users and highlight the importance of this call-to-action.

In future experimentation, we should consider the impact of visibility and ease of access to key calls-to-action on user engagement. By prioritizing important actions and making them more visible, we can potentially improve user interaction and achieve better results. It is important to continue testing and optimizing the user experience to ensure that the platform effectively encourages users to follow, share, and support those in need.

experiments.title: How humanized letter copy with photo alignment impacts revenue on main donate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanized letter copy with photo alignment for users on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion and revenue because it causes the main donation page to read like it’s from a real person, humanizing the experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2024 - 08/13/2024
For CaringBridge’s main donation page, we had tested a series of copy iteration on the main donation page that removed the photo background in favor of a cleaner design, with copy that was stylized to read like a humanized letter. This included a greeting, copy being updated so that it read like an address from Kelly, as well as her sign-off and photo a the bottom. Initially, this no difference when it was tested previously.
However, we hypothesized that by simply updating this letter copy treatment to also include Kelly’s photo aligned at the top of the letter versus at the bottom with her signature would help drive home the humanization of the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

1. Humanization of the donation page: By aligning Kelly's photo at the top of the humanized letter copy, the treatment created a more personal and engaging experience for users. This likely contributed to the significant increase in donor conversion and revenue.

2. Importance of personalized communication: Addressing users as if they were receiving a letter from Kelly added a personal touch to the donation page. This approach resonated well with users and led to a higher conversion rate.

3. Visual hierarchy matters: Placing Kelly's photo at the top of the letter helped to establish a visual hierarchy that drew users' attention to the humanized content. This likely played a role in driving the increased engagement and conversion.

4.Additionally, this also had the impact of acting as a social proof since in the copy Kelly also shares how she herself has been impacted by CaringBridge and donor generosity.

In conclusion, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of humanizing the donation experience and personalizing communication with donors. By leveraging these learnings and continuing to test and optimize the user experience, CaringBridge can further improve donor engagement and ultimately drive more significant impact for the nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How a Sticky Follow Button Impacts Site Follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky follow button that “pins” to the top of the page for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in site follows because it draws more attention to the button visually, but also highlights this as a primary call-to-action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2024 - 08/01/2024
On CaringBridge site profiles, we wanted to re-test a sticky follow button that stayed pinned to the page when a user scrolled down. This had previously been tested in Caring Bridge 1.o, but not yet in Caring Bridge 2.0.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a sticky follow button that stays pinned to the top of the page for users on a site profile led to a significant increase in site follows. The treatment version, which included the sticky follow button resulted in a 14.7% increase in Site Follows for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

A takeaway from this experiment is more than just making an element more visually apparent, but what that communicates to the reader. Of all the actions that can be taken on a site profile, we're highlighting this is one that's important.

This strategy can be applied to other features or buttons on the site to capture users' attention and encourage them to take the desired actions. Additionally, it is important to continue testing and iterating on design elements, language choices, and user experience to optimize engagement and achieve the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How relocating the Tribute widget to the right impacts tribute donations for JEN traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the tribute widget outside of the comment section pinned to the right for JEN users  will achieve an increase in tribute gifts because it better spotlights the ask while also keeping the two distinct CTA’s separate: comments and tribute gifts..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2024 - 07/22/2024
When a CaringBridge follows a new post/comment notification in email, they are brought to that post on a CaringBridge user’s profile. When they arrive, they are met with a Tribute widget directly in line with the comment section with an ask to make a tribute gift. Gifts to this tribute widget have been underperforming, and part of our hypothesis is that it’s due to there being two distinct CTA’s in the comment section. 1. CaringBridge wants the user to comment on the post. 2. But also the tribute widget presents a competing call to action. As a result, both can potentially be detracting from each other.
We developed two treatments to help address this where the Tribute Widget is pulled out of the comment section and placed on the right. We believed that this would better spotlight the ask, while also not interfering with the user experience when wanting to leave a comment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that placing the tribute widget outside of the comment section pinned to the right for JEN users resulted in a decrease in donations. Despite the hypothesis that this placement would better spotlight the ask and separate the two distinct calls to action, the treatment performed worse than the control.
There are items to consider around the execution of the experiment. For users in a smaller view desktop view, the widget would likely benefit from dynamically resizing as the user resizes their window. In some instances, it can be cut off or end up offscreen depending on the screen size.
The results of this experiment highlight the importance of monitoring and analyzing the impact of any changes made to the user interface or user experience to ensure that they are not detracting from the desired outcome. It may be beneficial to further investigate why the treatment performed poorly and iterate on the design before conducting another experiment.

experiments.title: How a pulling a donate button into the main navigation bar impacts clicks in mobile
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pulling the main donate button out of the dropdown menu and into the primary navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in clicks and traffic to the main donation page because it increases visibility of the call-to-action button and reduces the friction of having to “find” the donate button.. because  it increases visibility of the call-to-action button and reduces the friction of having to “find” the donate button.. because  it increases visibility of the call-to-action button and reduces the friction of having to “find” the donate button.. because  it increases visibility of the call-to-action button and reduces the friction of having to “find” the donate button..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
For CaringBridge users in mobile, we noticed that the current mobile experience removed the donate button from the top navigation and instead it was placed with a hamburger dropdown menu. Unless a user opened this menu, they would not see the main donate button. Our hypothesis was that by pulling this donate button out of the hidden hamburger menu and making it more visible for mobile users, would generate more clicks and drive more traffic to the main donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that making the main donate button more visible for mobile users, by pulling it out of the hidden hamburger menu and into the primary navigation bar, significantly increased clicks and traffic to the main donation page. The treatment saw a  3,409.9% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, proving that the hypothesis was correct.
Donor Conversion Rate:
Control: 69,032 Samples | 148 Donations | .2% Conv Rate |
Treatment: 69,165 Samples | 145 Donations | .2 Conv Rate | -2.2 Difference | 15% Significance
What this reveals is that although this didn't impact donor conversion, it is an effective way of driving more traffic to the page. In future, we can pivot our focus to improving conversion rate of the main donate page.
This experiment underscores the importance of testing and optimizing mobile user experiences to maximize engagement and conversions. It is crucial to continue monitoring user behavior, conducting A/B tests, and implementing changes based on data-driven insights to continually improve the effectiveness of the nonprofit's digital presence and achieve its goals.

experiments.title: How providing a micro ask of $25 for 2 weeks of impact, affects giving for onsite tribute widgets
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a microask  for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because it will lower the barrier to entry for prospective donors and reduce decision anxiety .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2024 - 03/24/2024
CaringBridge has a long-standing ask of $30 on their on-site tribute widgets. This was the approximate cost to power a site for a month several years ago but has since needed updating. In order to get closer to the actual cost to-date, and to reduce the barrier to entry for those who could not give higher amounts, we tested using a $25 ask that covers a site for 2 weeks (instead of a month). This was tested among multiple places on-site where a tribute widget appeared.
The first test ran (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/j6kp) was a $60 ask and that proved to be too high for site visitors – resulting in a valid 16% decrease in conversion rate and a decrease in revenue.
The second test ran  (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/gWqq) was a $50 ask that proved to also be too high for site visitors resulting in a 10% decrease in giving but flat revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that providing a microask for site visitors significantly increased donor conversion rates, demonstrating a 39.1% increase in donations for all traffic with a 99.9% level of statistical confidence. This confirms the hypothesis that offering a lower barrier to entry and reducing decision anxiety can positively impact giving behavior.
Furthermore, the experiment resulted in a substantial increase in revenue by 329% and a 385% increase in one-time donations, both with high levels of statistical confidence. This indicates that not only did more visitors donate, but they also contributed more in terms of monetary value.
Moving forward, these learnings should be used to inform future experimentation strategies. It is evident that adjusting the ask amount from $30 to a microask significantly impacted donor behavior in a positive way. Therefore, it would be beneficial to continue testing and optimizing donation asks to find the optimal balance between donor conversion rates and revenue generation. Additionally, considering other factors such as messaging, timing, and site placement in future experiments could further enhance donation outcomes.

experiments.title: How making the Follow Site button “sticky” so that it stays onscreen impacts site follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing visibility of the Follow button by making ti sticky for users on a CaringBridge profile will achieve an increase in site follows because it not only means they’re more likely to see it, but it highlights this feature as important.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2024 - 02/13/2024
For CaringBridge, site follows on a user profile is an important metric. It not only keeps friends and family better connected, it means they’re more likely to see the user’s posts and encounter key asks to support the site. In a previous iteration, we believed that highlighting this Follow button in the top right-hand corner with a bright orange color would make it stand out and more. This resulted in a huge increase in follows. For this iteration of the test, we hypothesized that taking this a step further and keeping the Follow Site button “sticky” so that when a user scrolls down the profile it stays in view on the right side-bar, would also increase visibility and site follows as a result.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

Making the Follow button sticky on a user's profile led to a significant increase in site followers. The treatment group experienced a 42.8% increase in Site Follows for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating a clear win for the treatment.

From this experiment, we learned the importance of increasing the visibility of key features such as the Follow button to drive user engagement. This should prompt future ideas about key metrics and how if we want a user to take them, we should make it visible and easy to do so. Exploring other potential sticky elements on the site that could enhance user experience and drive key metrics.

experiments.title: How increasing an ask from $30 to $60 impacts giving for onsite tribute widgets
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the ask amount for site visitors will achieve an increase in revenue because there is a larger initial ask that a site visitor is prompted by. because  there is a larger initial ask that a site visitor is prompted by.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2024 - 01/29/2024
CaringBridge has a long-standing ask of $30 on their on-site tribute widgets. This was the approximate cost to power a site for a month several years ago but has since needed updating. In order to get closer to the actual cost to-date, we tested using a $60 ask instead – knowing that we could potential lose donors but perhaps make up the difference in revenue by seeing a higher average gift. This was tested among multiple places on-site where a tribute widget appeared.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the ask amount for site visitors from $30 to $60 resulted in a significant decrease in donations. The treatment group, where the higher ask was used, saw a 16.2% decrease in donations compared to the control group.
This suggests that the higher initial ask of $60 may have deterred potential donors, leading to a decrease in the number of donations. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is 99.4%, indicating a high level of certainty in these results.
Additionally, because of the significant decrease in conversion rate, revenue did not increase as aspected. We saw a 9% decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 8.38%. It's suggested we test a lower amount so as not to double the ask right away. Or, prompt a specific ask on the donation page itself.
