experiments.title: How changing the color of a button impacts site follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the “Follow Site” button orange for site visitors will achieve an increase in follows because the visibility of the “Follow” CTA has increased.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/09/2023
In an effort to increase site follows, we decided to create a treatment that would draw more attention to it. The current button seemed to blend in with the design and might get lost when someone is scrolling through.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Making the "Follow Site" button orange increased its visibility and led to a 6.1% increase in site follows. This result was statistically significant with a 99.9% confidence level.

These findings indicate that the hypothesis was correct and that changing the color of the button to orange successfully drew more attention to it, resulting in higher engagement and more follows.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to consider the following:

Continually test different design elements to optimize user engagement. Color choice may have drawn more attention to it but there is more testing that can be done around the value proposition copy surrounding the button.

experiments.title: How removing a paragraph of language regarding an unsubscribe affects gifts given through a tribute widget
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the unsubscribe language for journal followers will achieve an increase in conversions from the journal entry page because removing the extraneous language increases clarity. .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/16/2023
CaringBridge drove a lot of emails from JEN notifications, which were sent when a user posted a new journal entry. There were two primary links in this email. The first linked to the journal entry, which users read the post and, at the end, were invited to give. The second was a direct ask to support CaringBridge.
At some point in the last few years, unsubscribe language was wedged in between these two links. CaringBridge wanted to remove this language, but wanted to understand how this had affected them in arrears.
Unfortunately, they were not able to A/B test this email as it was a transactional email. So, they decided to remove the language and look at the average traffic and conversion rate in the 30 days preceding the removal versus the average traffic and conversion rate in the 30 days after the removal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from the experiment is that removing extraneous language, such as unsubscribe language, can lead to an increase in conversions by increasing clarity for users. In this case, the treatment version without unsubscribe language saw a 16.4% increase in donations with a high level of statistical confidence, indicating that the removal of unsubscribe language had a significant impact.
In future experimentation, it may be worthwhile to consider removing extraneous language in other communications to increase clarity and potentially improve conversion rates. However, it is important to carefully track and measure the impact of any changes made to ensure that they are having the desired effect. Not all friction is bad friction!

experiments.title: How Yes/No “Autonomy” Language Impacts Clicks on a CYE Exit Intent
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that yes/no autonomy language for users on a CaringBridge visit profile will achieve increase in click-thru because users are more likely to interact with a call-to-action that presents an easy “micro-yes.”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2024
For CaringBridge’s CYE campaign, we hypothesized that Yes/No language on the call-to-action buttons would improve click-thru rate and send more traffic to the donation page. In the control, the call-to-action was “Donate Now” whereas in the treatment users were given a choice: “yes, I would like to give,” or “no, I’m not interested.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the hypothesis that yes/no autonomy language on the call-to-action buttons would improve click-thru rate and send more traffic to the donation page was proved to be incorrect. The treatment, which implemented the yes/no autonomy language, actually resulted in a 24.2% decrease in clicks for all traffic compared to the control.
This suggests that users were not more likely to interact with language that presents an easy "micro-yes" before being taken to the main donation page. In fact, it seems that the yes/no autonomy language may have confused or deterred users from clicking on the call-to-action buttons.
It should be noted that donor conversion rate wasn't captured for this experiment. It would be interesting to see the impacts downstream, whether less people making the commitment to "Yes, I would like to give" would still result in more conversions at the donation due to those users being more committed/motivated to give due to the micro-yes.

experiments.title: How social proof and communal language impacted donor conversion rate in an email update(Pt.2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using social proof and communal language for those receiving JEN emails will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because   people will identify with being a compassionate person and will want to join others who have donated.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2023
CaringBridge sends out what are called JEN asks. These are auto-generated emails that are sent out to those connected to sites. They provide journal updates and always include an ask in the email. We wanted to test using social proof/communal language to let donors know that they’re joining a larger group of compassionate people when they give. The last test we ran for this experiment generated a decrease in gifts. We had tested using fear of loss language so our hypothesis is that CaringBridge’s audience may respond more positively to joining in on something rather than missing out on something.
The first time we ran this experiment CaringBridge was undergoing a change in donation platform. This additional variable introduced during the experiment did not give us the clean data needed. We rested this concept to gather better data.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using social proof and communal language in JEN emails led to a significant decrease in donations for all traffic. This experiment resulted in a 34% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99% - making this a valid result. This confirms that the initial experiment is also a valid result in the decrease in donor conversion rate.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to explore other messaging strategies that may resonate better with the audience and use other psychological principles to move prospective donors to action.

experiments.title: How adding a tab for recurring tribute giving affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tab for a recurring donation for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring gifts with no decrease in overall donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 06/19/2023
Most of CaringBridge’s revenue came through their tribute donation page, which was driven by traffic to their journals. Because of the episodic nature of CaringBridge usage, donor retention was low — except for recurring donors. Since monthly recurring donors give smaller amounts regularly, they stick around a lot longer.
CaringBridge wanted to test including recurring giving on their donation page to see if it affected overall giving. They decided to allow it for 20% of traffic to mitigate any risk of decreasing overall giving. They did this via a “tabbed” form that gave the user a new “first choice” between a one-time and a recurring gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, the treatment produced no statistically significant change in giving, while producing 8 recurring gifts from a very small slice of the traffic. It is interesting that the treatment drove a directional 3.7% lift in overall giving, which might suggest that the tab decision (which is a "smaller decision" than the gift array, increased overall engagement with the donation form.
This shows CaringBridge that they can safely include a recurring option without affecting overall giving, and produce valuable recurring donors from this funnel.

experiments.title: How a Pop-Up on the Journal Page Increased Follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup with value copy for readers on a user’s page, but who do not follow them will achieve an increase in follows because the copy helps impart the importance of staying updating and how they can offer support by following.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2023 - 04/07/2023
CaringBridge is a platform where users can update friends and family about their health journey. In the journal view of a user’s page, CaringBridge wanted to capture users who were browsing the user’s entries but did not follow them.  We hypothesized that a simple popup with value copy about staying updating and informed on the user’s health journey would increase the number of follows.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a pop-up with value copy about staying updated and informed on the user's health journey can significantly increase the number of follows on a user's page. The treatment group saw a 138.8% increase in Follow Site (Journal Pages) for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This improvement was seen across all visitor types, including desktop, mobile, tablet, new, returning, direct, email, organic search, and paid search visitors. However, visitors aged 45-54 had a 221% decrease in follow site (journal pages) with a 97% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the age group that did not respond positively to the pop-up and adjust the messaging accordingly. Additionally, testing different variations of the pop-up, such as other types of value copy or presenting it at various points during the user's journey on the platform, could reveal even more effective ways to increase follows. Overall, the results of this experiment demonstrate the value of reminding users, not only that they weren't already following, but also the important of how their follow supports the user.

We had tested using fear of loss language so our hypothesis is that CaringBridge’s audience may respond more positively to joining in on something rather than missing out on something.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Before going through results, it is worth noting that CaringBridge underwent a change in donation platform on the same day that this experiment was launched. Therefore, the results of this experiment were compromised and it should be launched again to confirm results.
The key learning from this experiment is that using social proof and communal language in JEN asks did not result in an increase in donor conversion rate for CaringBridge. In fact, the treatment resulted in a significant decrease in donations compared to the control. We saw a valid 58% decrease in donations resulting in a level of confidence of 99%.
This suggests that CaringBridge's audience may not respond well to communal language and instead, may require a different approach to encourage donations. However, given the change in an additional variable (the donation platform), it's recommended we retest this approach.

experiments.title: How a pop up to prompt site follows performs against a full page interruption
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the full site interrupter for site visitors will achieve a decrease in both site creations and donations because the full purple curtain seems much more disruptive to the person’s site experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 04/29/2023
CaringBridge had previously implemented a full page, purple interrupter that prompts the user to follow the author’s site. We noticed a decrease in donations at the time and hypothesized that this new feature was the culprit. While the call to action on this interrupter is for site follows, it shows up on a page that generates a lot of gifts. To confirm our hypothesis we ran this as an A/B test where the control was the regular pop up for site follows and the treatment was the “purple curtain” pop up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by Caringbridge revealed that the full page site interrupter for site visitors decreased the site follows by 16.5%. The statistical level of confidence was 100%, indicating that the control won. Moreover, the experiment resulted in a directional 64% increase in donations with a level of confidence of 87%. However, when drilling down to where these gifts are coming from we actually found that the donations had a split code that was completely unrelated to this test experience.
The key learning from this experiment is that full-site interrupters could be disruptive to user experience, leading to a reduction in site creation and potentially donations.

experiments.title: How fear of loss language impacted donor conversion rate in an email update
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using fear of loss language for those receiving JEN emails will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because   people will be motivated to ensure that others don’t lose their sites and communities.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2023
CaringBridge sends out what are called JEN asks. These are auto-generated emails that are sent out to those connected to sites. They provide journal updates and always include an ask in the email. We wanted to test using fear of loss language to communicate to prospective donors what happens when they don’t give a gift: people don’t have a site and don’t have the community they need when going through their health journeys. We hypothesized that this additional context would increase donor conversion rate through this email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using fear of loss language did not increase donor conversion rate and actually resulted in a decrease in donations. The control version that focused on appreciation for staying connected and powering the site for updates was more effective in motivating donors. The experiment resulted in a valid 19% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 95%.
In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing different messaging that focuses on the benefits of donating rather than the negative consequences of not donating.

experiments.title: How anniversary language and monthly donor language affect clickthrough rate on a monthly giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding anniversary language and changing “partner” to “monthly donor” for return visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
CaringBridge had used popups to ask repeat visitors to consider giving a monthly gift. While the performance had built a file of recurring donors, they wanted to see if they could increase the clickthrough rate and conversion rate to monthly donor. They formed two hypotheses:
Using language highlighting their 25th anniversary would increase relevance and clickthrough rate and conversion rate
Using “monthly donor” rather than “CaringBridge partner” would increase the clarity of the ask and increase conversion rate.
They decided to test this with an A/B/C test. Their first treatment introduced the 25th anniversary language, but kept the “partner” language. The second kept the 25th anniversary language, but introduced “monthly donor” in place of “partner”.
They launched this to determine a winning variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
They found that, on the basis of clickthrough rate, the added relevance of the 25th anniversary mention increased clickthrough rate by 16.2% with an 88.9% level of confidence. However, the combination of the 25th anniversary language and the "monthly donor" language increased clickthrough rate by 44.9%.
Previous testing had shown that an increase in clickthrough rate did not always correlate to more recurring donors, so they needed to also examine downstream data to see how that traffic converted.

experiments.title: How clarifying the call-to-action affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity of the call to action while adding language to increase the “fear of loss” increase donor conversion rate over the existing treatment emphasizing the more general ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2014 - 11/25/2014
The Tribute widget drive the majority of Caringbridge’s organic site revenue. It is small advertisement on a patient’s journal page that asks the visitor to make a tribute gift. With the volume of Caringbridge’s site traffic, improving the conversion rate of this widget would have an exponential impact.
A treatment was developed with the goal of clarifying the purpose of the gift. The idea was that if we can have visitors better understand the purpose of the gift, they will be more likely to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The resulting treatment made multiple impacts along the path to conversion. By itself, the new widget language increased the number of clicks by 19.3%.

Not only that but this increased traffic was more qualified than the visitors that the control would drive since they had a better understanding of the purpose of their gift. As a result, the visitors to the subsequent page had a 52% increase in conversion and a 54% decrease in refunds.

The cumulative effect of all of these lifts resulted in an 85.9% increase in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the call to action to “create a page” for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in site creation rate because we are reducing the potential anxiety someone experiences when being asked to create a site. because  we are reducing the potential anxiety someone experiences when being asked to create a site.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 04/22/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the call to action from “create a site” to “create a page” had a significant impact in increasing the website creation rate. The treatment group saw a valid 151.4% increase in website creation for all traffic, with a statistical level of confidence of 99.0%.
These results indicate that asking someone to "create a page" reduces any potential anxiety they may have had in taking action. Creating page sounds like it's less time consuming and manageable.
Our proposed next experiment is using the world "journal" instead of "page." We believe that the word "journal" lowers the barrier to entry even further.

experiments.title: How removing a paragraph of language regarding an unsubscribe affects gifts given through a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the unsubscribe language for journal followers will achieve an increase in conversions from the direct link because removing the extraneous language increases clarity..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/16/2023
CaringBridge drove a lot of emails from JEN notifications, which were sent when a user posted a new journal entry. There were two primary links in this email. The first linked to the journal entry, which users read the post and, at the end, were invited to give. The second was a direct ask to support CaringBridge.
At some point in the last few years, unsubscribe language was wedged in between these two links. CaringBridge wanted to remove this language, but wanted to understand how this had affected them in arrears.
Unfortunately, they were not able to A/B test this email as it was a transactional email. So, they decided to remove the language and look at the average traffic and conversion rate in the 30 days preceding the removal versus the average traffic and conversion rate in the 30 days after the removal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing unsubscribe language for journal followers had a significant positive impact on donations for all traffic. The treatment achieved a 62.3% increase in donations with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that removing the extraneous language increased clarity and helped users focus on the call-to-action.
This particularly affected the direct ask, as it was positioned below the unsubscribe language. However, it also positively affected the traffic and conversion from the journal link, which showed that this extra paragraph negatively affected both the copy above it and below it.

experiments.title: How a monthly-first tabbed donation form affected monthly gift conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the one-time recurring decision above the donation amount decision and pre-selecting the recurring option for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/16/2023
CaringBridge’s main donation page received a steady but low volume of donations. They wanted to test ideas that would convert more of these gifts, which were mostly episodic gifts, into monthly recurring gifts. The monthly selection was below the donation amount selection, and they hypothesized that they needed to move the “one-time or monthly” selection above the gift amount. They also decided to make the donation form monthly by default, so that the user would select to move to a one-time gift. They launched a test of these two changes to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment was that moving the one-time recurring decision above the donation amount decision and pre-selecting the recurring option for main donation page visitors led to a significant increase in donation conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 339.3% increase in donations compared to the control group. This was statistically significant with a confidence level of 99.9%.
These results suggest that making it easier for donors to choose a recurring donation option and pre-selecting it by default can lead to higher conversion rates.
In analyzing this experiment, the team wondered if the word "recurring" was more confusing than "monthly", and set up a test to better understand the difference in nomenclature.

experiments.title: How introducing a felt need affects clickthrough rate on site start remarketing ads
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing the “why” for potential site creators will achieve an increase in click thru rate  because we are addressing their personal needs and providing our services as the solution. . because  we are addressing their personal needs and providing our services as the solution.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/14/2023
CaringBridge was running remarketing ads in Facebook in order to recapture traffic that may have been interested to start a new site with CaringBridge. In an effort to increase click thru rate we proposed 3 different ad variations with 3 different motivations. We wanted to see which of these motivations would resonate more with their audiences.
The first treatment highlighted the privacy that a CaringBridge site offers. The second treatment addresses the felt need for the individual and how a CaringBridge site keeps you connected to others. The third treatment emphasizes how you can journal your progress on the site to continue getting support with others.
All three ads ran congruently on Facebook with the original remarketing ad which focused more on efficiency.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The winning variant for this experiment was treatment #2 with a valid 31.8% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 95.1% level of confidence. The other two ads had almost no difference in click thru rates. This informs us that treatment #2 more effectively connected with the reader's felt need with the first sentence of the ad being: "The moments you need help most are precisely when its hardest to ask for it." This is a sentence that introduces empathy and gets to the heart of why someone needs support in their health journey. In fact the first treatment may have introduced a level of anxiety because of the mention of publicly sharing their personal life.
It's recommended that we test similar language on the landing page itself in order to increase new site creations after people click through so that the messaging of their journey is consistent.

experiments.title: How sending journal traffic to a high-converting journal page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening journal entries to summaries on the journal listing page for site visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will send more traffic to a higher-converting page. because  it will send more traffic to a higher-converting page. because  it will send more traffic to a higher-converting page. because  it will send more traffic to a higher-converting page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/02/2023
CaringBridge had done lots of previous testing on their “journal listing” page, a page that was commonly accessed from the navigation section of a patient’s website. They had steadily increased the conversion rate on this page, but it consistently underperformed the “journal view” section of the site, an individual journal entry that had a “tribute widget” on the page.
The journal listing page put the full text of every journal entry available for reading, which made for a very long page. The team hypothesized that, if they truncated the posts and added a “read more” link that set the user to the “journal view” page, they would be able to get more traffic to a better reading experience with a higher converting tribute widget at the end of each post.
This was a highly technical test to set up, but they were able to create an A/B experiment to see which one drove more conversions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening journal entries to summaries on the journal listing page for site visitors can lead to an increase in conversion rate, because it sends more traffic to a higher-converting page. This was evident from the 131.6% increase in donations for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence achieved by the treatment.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue to test and iterate on the journal listing page, as well as other pages on the website, to identify opportunities for improving the user experience and increasing conversion rates. Additionally, it is important to ensure that any changes made are backed up by data and rigorous testing to ensure that they are effective and beneficial for the organization.

CaringBridge was running their holiday appeal, which started with a Giving Tuesday ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that asking for a range of amounts from previous donors can lead to an increase in conversion rate.
Specifically, the experiment achieved a 36.0% increase in donations for all traffic with a 100% statistical level of confidence.
In the future, we can use this knowledge to focus on tailoring donation asks to different donor segments and understanding which ranges appeal best to each. Additionally, this experiment gives us a model to use when designing future optimized donation pages. Finally, it also highlights the importance of experimentation in understanding donor behaviors. This experience can be leveraged to refine experiments in the future to uncover new insights and maximize donation growth.

experiments.title: How offering a lower gift amount affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a $25 gift amount to the array for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 11/10/2022
CaringBridge was curious as to how they could increase conversion rate on their tribute donation pages. As inflation had increased as a significant downward impact on the economy, they wondered if their initial ask amount of $50 was too high. They decided to test this by adding a $25 amount as a new lower barrier of entry.
They split the traffic to determine the impact of this new low ask amount on conversion and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the lower ask amount produced an 8% decrease in conversion rate, which also delivered less revenue due to a lower average gift. As they looked at the device segments, they noticed this was heavily weighed down by mobile traffic, which had a 10% decrease and made up more than 60% of the traffic.
Upon reviewing the mobile experience, they noticed a slight anomaly with the way the array laid out on mobile with four options, so they decided to test a slightly different design in a new test, just for mobile, to see if redesigning the options could affect conversion rate.

experiments.title: How communal language affects clickthrough rate on a recurring giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for repeat visitors will achieve an increase in monthly donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/09/2021
CaringBridge had run a popup asking visitors on their 2nd and 8th visits to become a recurring donor for more than a year, which had built a strong base of monthly donors. The control asked donors to “consider something” before they go, and then talked about the need for recurring donors. They decided to try to include some “communal language”, that first thanked the donor for staying connected to the CaringBridge community and then solicited them for a monthly gift based on their firsthand experience of the value of the CaringBridge community.
They decided to test this new language through an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The communal language on the popup increased clickthrough rate by 18.7%. However, upon reviewing the conversion rate on the donation page from the two popup treatments, it was clear that the communal language attracted more clicks but decreased the motivation of the donors, as conversion rate dropped 75.2% from the increase in clicks. This is a reminder that all clicks are not created equal—more traffic does not necessarily result in more donors if those donors are not properly motivated and incentivized by the existing copy.

CaringBridge had a secondary hypothesis that the language on the donation page was not robust enough, and, with some improvement to the page copy, might be able to convert more of these "communal language clickers". They decided to run a monthly giving donation page test first and then re-test this with the winner.

experiments.title: How communal-based communication affects clickthrough rate on a matching gift popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language in the donation popup for website visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2020
CaringBridge was running a two-day match in the month of December as part of their year end campaign. They had previously tested into a highly effective popup during matches that used urgency and an hourly donor countdown to increase response during these time-sensitive campaigns. However, they wondered if more “communal” messaging that affirmed the donor in their relationship to the CaringBridge author and made a less direct ask would be more effective at getting people to enter the donation funnel.
They drafted a treatment that made some key shifts. First, it began by talking about the donor themselves—”You’ve been such a great friend by staying engaged through CaringBridge.” Then, it softened the ask by starting with “If it’s not too much to ask”, which gives the donor a sort of “release valve” from the pressure of “donate now”. Finally, the matching gift from the Board of Directors was positioned as an opportunity or service offering provided to the donor rather than something they were expected to respond to.
They split the traffic between the two popups and monitored downstream traffic separately to see how it affected both clickthrough rate and conversion, as they realized that the two do not always correlate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language produced a 45.3% increase in clickthrough rate. There are a few reasons behind what might have contributed to the increase.

First, the intro of "You've been such a great friend by staying connected through CaringBridge" not only affirms the donor, it piques their interest. It's a compliment, rather than the presumptive "your donation will be matched" language on the control. This additional engagement could have contributed to more people reading the next sentence—"If it's not too much to ask, would you consider giving today so that our Board of Directors can match your gift?".

This next sentence presents the ask in a completely different way. First, the donor is given an initial choice: "is this too much to ask, or not?", before they read about the incentive. Second, the incentive is offered in a radically different way than the control. Where the control actually thanks the Board of Directors for matching, the treatment gives the donor the chance to be the hero by making the gift, and places the match in a position that is responsive to the donor's action.

This test illuminated the possibilities of "communal" language for CaringBridge—which is a very "communal" organization in the first place. This opens up the opportunity do to more testing on the donation page downstream from this ask.

				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How tangible donation language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a tangible “handle” for giving increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2017 - 04/06/2017
CaringBridge had done some message development around the value proposition of a donation. Donations to CaringBridge help power a platform that helps people stay connected. Their value proposition centered around this connection, as multiple tests had proven. However, they hypothesized that adding a more tangible offer for the donor into the ask might increase conversion. They calculated that it takes $30 to power a single CaringBridge site for a month, and developed a treatment of their tribute widget (which appears on the most highly-trafficked sections of their site) that called this out.
Then, they launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the tangible connection to the gift amount, produced a 34.3% increase in donations. This showed that while the emotional “connection-based” language had been (and was still) effective at attracting donations, there was a segment of users who needed to better understand what their gift did—and how their choice to donate could have a “tangible” impact when the entire platform they were supporting was virtual.
This prompted more testing, specifically around how to leverage this messaging in the gift array.

experiments.title: How increasing the number of hyperlinks in an email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing the number of links in an email increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2016 - 01/16/2017
The treatment produced a 27% increase in clickthrough rate, which tells us a few things about our readers. First, it tells us that including more links incentivizes more of them to click, or take the first initial action at the moment that the value proposition hits home. Secondly, it tells us that we had previously been losing potential donors who didn’t make it all the way through the copy to the call-to-action.
This points to an important principle: our donors and prospects have varying levels of motivation. Some are ready to give and need very little convincing, while others are more deliberative and might need more copy to be incentivized to click. Typically, we attempt to address these varying levels of motivation through multiple emails, but this experiment points to the opportunity that we might be able to reach multiple levels of motivation in the same communication.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had always included a “focused link” in their email appeals, which gave readers a single link to click to take action. Historically, this had been a button, but previous testing had shown that a text link performed better.
They wanted to test the effect of giving donors more places to click throughout the email—essentially linking up any text that contained a CTA, so if the donor was motivated, they could take action right then. They produced a treatment that had the exact same copy, but included more clickable links.

experiments.keyLearnings:
The communal language on the popup increased clickthrough rate by 18.7%. However, upon reviewing the conversion rate on the donation page from the two popup treatments, it was clear that the communal language attracted more clicks but decreased the motivation of the donors, as conversion rate dropped 75.2% from the increase in clicks. experiments.title: How specificity affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does broadening the recipient of the fundraising ask affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2015 - 03/14/2015
CaringBridge was launching a two-week fundraising campaign and wanted to run a test on the tribute donation pages for each Journal Site. Because traffic from tribute pages is generated from a specific person’s Journal, the CaringBridge team wanted to test the impact of making an ask just for that person versus an ask to support the entire CaringBridge community. The result would deliver valuable learnings for future fundraising campaigns and landing page tests.
experiments.keyLearnings: The broader ask introduced in the treatment reduced donor conversion rate by 17.6%. This told the team that people who begin the donation process from an individual’s page are more motivated by honoring their friend or loved one than by supporting the entire community.

experiments.title: How clarity of the value proposition through cause and effect copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will clarifying the value proposition in the headline using cause and effect copy increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 05/26/2016
The treatment produced a 21.1% increase in donor conversion. This tells us that by introducing the effect of the gift before highlighting the cause (the gift from the donor), we can increase the likelihood that the visitor will make a donation.
Too often, we ask people to donate without reminding them of the impact of their gift. This test prompts further testing of ordering of the statements to see if we can increase clarity and further increase results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The majority of CaringBridge’s donations come through their tribute donation pages, where visitors can give support CaringBridge in honor of a friend or loved one. A previous test had shown the effect of organization-centric copy on donor conversion, and the team wanted to build on that learning. They proposed a test to the value proposition in the headline copy using “cause and effect” language.
The control headline had been tested many times — “You make [tribute name]’s site possible”. The team proposed moving this to the subheadline, and highlighting why the site exists in the main headline: “This website helps [Tribute name] stay connected to family and friends”.
Then, the original headline would assign responsibility (or credit) to the visitor below that copy.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How clarity and ordering on a donation form affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will ordering the donation steps and clarifying language increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2016 - 08/04/2016
The treatment with the ordered copy produced a 12.5% lift in donations, which proved that the user testing had unlocked a powerful principle. While CaringBridge saw their donation process as one big step, the donors responded positively when they were told what it took to complete each step with numerical ordering and increased clarity.
This is a powerful reminder that we must learn to see our donation processes through the eyes of the donor to properly assist them and markedly improve conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had done some user testing in anticipation of launching a new site experience. During that testing, a few users asked questions that suggested that the donation process was unclear and could use clarity and ordering. Based on these responses, they developed a treatment that numbered the header copy in the donation steps, used more personal and descriptive language, and increased perceived security around the donor’s information.
In the first header, they changed “Make your donation” to “1. Choose your donation amount”. Then, they added a number to the next call-to-action, “Write a Message of Support”. Finally, they changed the header “Your Information” to “3. Enter Your Information (It’s secure)”, which was designed to add clarity and reduce anxiety.
They launched an A/B test to quantify the impact.

experiments.title: How removing third-party quotes affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does third-party credibility decrease conversion when the primary value proposition is a personal experience?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/28/2016 - 03/10/2016
This is a powerful test that has implications for future appeals and fundraising campaigns. While the power of story can be a compelling addition to a fundraising ask, it might get in the way for a site like CaringBridge that serves as the conduit for person-to-person interactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had featured quotes from a family who used the site on their donation pages for a monthlong campaign. After the campaign was over, the quotes stayed on the donation pages.
However, previous testing had shown that since CaringBridge is a very personal experience, and response often decreased when a third-party family (that was unfamiliar to the user) was introduced.
We developed a treatment page that removed the third-party quote and ran an A/B split test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How adding suggested gift amounts affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the number of options on the donation form increase the number of conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2015 - 02/17/2015
The increased choice led to a 104.4% increase in donor conversion. Additionally there was only a $4 decrease in average gift (which was not significant or valid), so there was a significant increase in revenue overall as well. This suggested several other tests to erase the dip in average gift and maintain conversion, but was immediately rolled out to other forms on the site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had 3 suggested gift amounts on their donation page for as long as anyone could remember: $70, $125, and $300. These numbers had been tested in the past, but no one on the current team had any reference point for their selection. Data showed that a large number of donors used the “other” field to enter their gift amount (mostly lower gifts around $20). We thought that increasing the number of suggested amounts might decrease decision friction and increase conversion rate for those who wanted to give, but thought that the initial ask was too high. Our only fear was that we might lower average gift by lowering the initial ask, so we hoped that the increase in conversions made up for the perceived decrease.
We launched a new treatment with 6 giving options: $25, $50, $75, $100, $250, and $500, as well as the other field.

experiments.title: How reducing donation form friction affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does reducing the visual length of a donation form reduce friction enough to improve conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2015 - 03/07/2015
The main donation form on Caringbridge’s site had historically used the out-of-the-box styling options that came with their donation form widget.  This styling had each field stretch the full width of the screen with each field on a separate line.  This layout created a long page that had the impression of requiring a great deal of information.
Our hypothesis was that the increased form length created cognitive friction in the donor’s mind which ultimately would be affecting the conversion rate.  As a result, we wanted to rearrange the fields to create the impression of a shorter form.
experiments.keyLearnings: With the rearranged form creating a 39% increase in donor conversion, it is evident that the friction created in the mind of the donor by the long page was affecting performance. In addition to utilizing shorter forms in the future, we will also look for other ways to minimize the visual impact of the donation form.

experiments.title: How visually increasing urgency affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which combination of elements creates the greatest sense of urgency that allows for the increase in conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/24/2014 - 12/28/2014
CaringBridge was in the final week of their 2014 year-end fundraising campaign and expected traffic to dramatically increase to their donation pages. They wanted to maximize on-page conversion by adding elements to the page to increase urgency: reminding people of the proximity to December 31st.
We knew that adding elements like a countdown clock and a thermometer would increase urgency, but we tested to see which element (or combination of elements) would maximize landing page conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock was able to increase the urgency of the ask which, in turn, lead to the increase in conversion rate. However, when combined with the thermometer element of the campaign, it negatively affected the conversion rate. This may be due to the large goal seeming out of reach for the average donor. Whatever the case, for large campaigns like this, the countdown clock will be deployed in the future to increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How additional tactics improved giving on top of an already powerful match appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email will result in the greatest amount of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2018 - 04/02/2018
In past experiments, we have discovered a powerful motivating factor: the family story. And it makes sense: to the donor, it makes the benefit more concrete and it is presented in a way that human beings in general are best likely to absorb and process (story).
The lingering question, however, is to what end?  Is it possible to combine motivating factors like this family story with a match and to see an additive-like effect on total donations?
Especially for email?
experiments.keyLearnings:
At first glance, presenting the benefits of donating in the form of a story, and in a concrete example (clarity of value, construal theory), can have an additive motivating effect, even among match appeals where the motivation would presumably be high enough to begin with.
But that’s not all…
If you look at the sender, you have a CEO (less likely to be a genuine email) versus an actual CaringBridge User (more likely to be perceived genuine.
You also have more emphasis on a communal relationship rather than an exchange based relationship (i.e “your investment”).  Academic fundraising research has shown that major donors have been found to have more communal relationships with non-profits, and that communal strength and prosocial behavior is strongly correlated.
So it is possible that it was not just the story approach that helped propel the win, it was the combination of these other items as well:

experiments.title: How additional value proposition copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the amount of value proposition copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2017 - 03/20/2017
The treatment produced a 26.7% increase in donor conversion from the control, which showed that the amount of copy in the email is not as important as what it says. Not all words are created equal.
CaringBridge had done extensive testing in the area of personal tone—that is, getting the emails to read like an actual person wrote them. However, this concept needs to be balanced with the principle of value proposition. If the copy maintains a personal tone but does not convey enough value, then it’s just a nice communication piece.
This experiment is a good check against the concept of “long copy vs. short copy”—the most important concept is to get the right copy, regardless of length.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge sent a fundraising email to their housefile as part of a monthly appeal. The email told a personal story from a mother who had experienced the power of CaringBridge firsthand. Their control email told a compelling personal story using a convincing personal tone, but they thought it lacked the value proposition copy necessary to compel readers to give.
They created a treatment that increased the amount of copy significantly, but provided (what they perceived to be) a stronger value proposition. They believed that even though this increased the time it took to read the email, that the additions would produce a greater return.
They split their file in half and launched an experiment to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How the design of a fundraising email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stripped-down fundraising email increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2016 - 03/30/2016
The stripped-down email (with no template) produced an 80% increase in clickthrough rate. This tells us something about trust — and giving is an act of trust. It may imply that people are more likely to take action when they feel that the email is coming from a person, rather than an organization.
There were still elements of the treatment email that weren’t entirely personal — the big blue button, and the logo. This prompted further testing to see if the removal of these elements produced an additional lift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge received a matching gift for their spring fundraising campaign from a board member. Since the majority of their audience was unfamiliar with the board member, Rik Lalim, they originally created a templated email that was explicitly “CaringBridge” to ease users’ potential anxiety. But they had previously tested emails that had no formatting and emulated “normal” email, and experienced great success. They decided to create a treatment version that stripped away all the formatting and photography.
They then split their email file and ran a test to see which attracted more responses

The treatment subject line, despite the unfamiliar sender, produced a 137.2% increase in open rate. This implies that personalization in the subject line is a powerful trigger that can effectively bridge a gap between an unknown sender and a recipient.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge was running a fundraising campaign around Mother’s Day. Their email appeals typically came from Sona Mehring, their founder.
As part of a Mother’s Day fundraising campaign, they wanted to test a different approach, sending an email from a user of the site. They hypothesized that this personal approach would result in more donations. However, they knew they would have to bridge the relational gap between the new sender and the recipient, and they believed this could be done by maximizing personalization.
Their new treatment, included personalization in the subject line with the first name of the recipient. It also included personalization in the first few lines of the copy, which came through in the preview text.
They split the file and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How a personal tone affects donations in an email fundraising appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radically more personal email increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2016 - 03/22/2016
The treatment generated a 145.5% lift in donations. This shows, along with several other tests, that personal tone can have a radical effect on fundraising results. As marketers, we spend too much time crafting a message engineered to deliver the desired result, without remembering the way that people actually write emails. When we remember that fundraising is about relationships, we can use empathy to actually communicate with our donors.
Email is so much more than a “one-to-many” tool — it’s something that most people use every day to stay in touch, and it gives us the ability to give that personal experience to thousands of people at the same time.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge sent several email fundraising appeals as part of a campaign. Their team had the second appeal ready to go, a personal letter from one of the development team members. However, they hypothesized that they could increase results by removing the parts of the email that stood out as “marketing” and increasing the personal tone of the email copy.
The control had a logo at the top, which stood out as something that normal personal emails don’t have. It also had a call-to-action button, which rarely, if ever are used in personal emails. But the biggest shift was in the copy tone. The control was written from a detached third-person point of view that didn’t feel like someone sat down at a computer and wrote it.
They created a treatment that moved the logo to the signature area, replaced the button with a text link, and drastically changed the copy. The treatment copy started by acknowledging

experiments.title: How increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/31/2018
CaringBridge’s marketing team sought to increase the amount of users who got to their “ways to help” page, where there were several options to deepen engagement and provide more support to their friend or family member.
They thought that a slight copy change would align the “Ways to help” button on the journal page with the user’s intent. Instead of “Ways to help”, they proposed “Help [FirstName]” to encourage more people to click through. They thought this might work because they hypothesized that people who were reading this page were (consciously or unconsciously) desiring to help their friend or family member.
They were concerned about possible reactions to this test, so they only included 10% of site traffic in the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new copy produced a 19% lift in clickthrough rate for the “Ways to help” page—an impressive lift considering the small change. This points to more opportunities to align site copy with user intent throughout the site. With the volume of traffic that CaringBridge has, there is tremendous potential to increase clarity and adjust user experience accordingly.

experiments.title: How isolating a quantified ask affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: When isolated, what impact does the quantified ask have on clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2017 - 03/27/2017
The first treatment, with just the quantified ask, produced a 26% increase in clickthrough rate. However, in further testing, it failed to produce any increase in conversion and produced a spike in refunds.
The second treatment, with the headline change only, produced a 10% decrease in clickthrough rate, which showed that the cumulative lift in previous testing had more correlation with the presence of the quantified ask, but was boosted by the headline as well.
This prompted further testing to see if they could clarify the message on the donation page to reduce confusion and refund rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge tested a radical redesign that produced a big lift in donor conversion. This was a valuable finding, but they wanted further clarity on the lift, since two factors were changed. In the first experiment, they changed the headline of their tribute widget and added a “quantified ask”, with the offer that a $30 gift would keep their friend or loved one’s site online for a month.
They were curious which element had the most impact between these two. So they created two treatments: one that reverted to the old headline and included the quantified ask, and one that reverted to the old body copy (without the quantified ask), but kept the winning headline.
They launched an A/B/C test to validate a winner and determine the impact of each element on conversion.

experiments.title: How different value propositions affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will convert the most donors on the JEN donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2018
One of the essential services that CaringBridge provides is sending free email notifications when a user updates their journal. These are internally called “JEN” emails—Journal Email Notifications. Each JEN email contains a donation ask, which leads straight to a donation page. The copy on the JEN donation page had never been brought in line with other copy, so the team decided to test a value proposition that had worked elsewhere in the site, as well as a new value proposition on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The "tribute" copy, taken from the tribute page produced a 23% decrease in conversion, though the result was not statistically valid. However, the "email copy" variant, which took copy from the email and put it on the donation page, produced a statistically valid 35% decrease. 

This shows two interesting lessons—what works best in one area of the site will not necessarily work best in another, especially when the user is driven from an email instead of another page within the site. However, with less than 10% of users completing a transaction on this page, there is still plenty of optimization to be done.

experiments.title: How increasing intensity of color on a widget affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the intensity of the tribute widget attract more attention and increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2017 - 10/25/2017
CaringBridge had long sought to increase clickthrough rate on their tribute widget, which started the gift process for . One of their designers hypothesized that the current tribute widget blended in too much with the rest of the site and needed some “pop”. Using the intense CaringBridge brand purple, she created a treatment designed to attract more attention, and hopefully clickthrough rate.
CaringBridge tested the new tribute widget in both desktop and mobile views and launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment tribute widget reduced clickthrough rate by 27.6%, which further reduced donations and revenue downstream. We believe this can largely be attributed to the decrease in readability due to the white text now being placed on the intense purple.  In breaking down the data by device, most of the decrease came from mobile devices, in which this effect is intensified due to the smaller screen size.
This was just the first test in a series of tribute widget tests designed to increase clickthrough rate.

experiments.title: How streamlining the tribute widget affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a streamlined widget with added social proof inspire more people to begin the donation process?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2015 - 04/24/2015
Most gifts to CaringBridge start with the “tribute widget”, a small box that appears on all Site Journal pages and profile pages. We noticed one day that the tribute widget had a lot of information that could be extraneous and distracting from the intended action, which was to begin the gift process.
The CaringBridge team developed a streamlined version of this widget that removed a “most recent quote”, along with avatars for signed in users. To increase clarity, the team put pre-filled text into the box to instruct the user what to write. Also, we added social proof into the widget, dynamically pulling the number of previous donors to inspire the user to join them.
experiments.keyLearnings: The streamlined widget with added social proof resulted in nearly 52% more people beginning the donation process. We closely monitored donation page conversion rates as well, and there was no change. Clarifying the value proposition by removing extra graphics and copy and reducing anxiety through social proof resulted in a significant lift in dollars raised.

experiments.title: How security indicators on a donation form affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding visual elements increase the perception of form security increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/12/2017
The treatment with the additional visual indicators of security produced a 14.4% lift in donor conversion, which showed that there was a segment of donors who were convinced to complete their transaction with the extra reassurance that their transaction was secure.
What’s interesting is that the design of the CaringBridge form puts this test far below the “fold” of the page, which indicates that some visitors might have actually started the donation process and “bailed out” because they didn’t feel that their transaction would be secure. This suggests further testing to increase the perception of security earlier in the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had run a series of tests on their donation forms to significantly increase conversion over a period of three years. However, they proposed a new test, after reading a research study that showed that the most common reason web visitors didn’t complete transactions online was a perceived lack of security.
They wondered if their donation form was losing potential donors because it didn’t visually represent how secure it was. So, they created a special security box around the credit card section of form and added a padlock into the shaded box to represent that the form was secure. This didn’t make the form more secure—it was already PCI compliant and had the highest levels of security. All these design tweaks did was increase perception of security with the end user. They launched an A/B test to see if it increased donor conversion as they hypothesized.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2015 - 11/05/2015
This tells us that a more direct ask, even if it contains less personalization, presents a clearer value proposition that results in increased conversions over the treatment.
The goal of the donation page is to increase the perceived value to the donor. While the treatment sought to do this, the decrease in clarity caused by the added text negated any improvement in value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: CaringBridge wanted to improve the clarity of the value proposition on their tribute donation pages, which are landing pages that are personalized to the person whose journal they have been reading. They wanted to see if improved clarity and prioritized personalization would improve conversion rate. They created a treatment that connected the donor’s gift back to the person, though it took more words to explain.

This suggests that CaringBridge hit on some powerful language that hits home with their donors, and the match language, while providing an incentive, might actually complicate the value proposition.
This experiment suggests more testing around the quantified ask.

They had run this experiment as part of a match, so they wanted to re-run the experiment without the matching language to see if that impacted conversion.
The control asked visitors to “honor” their friend or loved one with a donation. The treatment used much more straightforward language: “Can you help power their site?” It also incorporated a quantified ask: “$30 keeps their site online for a month.”
They set up an A/B test to determine a winner in a non-match scenario.

experiments.title: The effect of a reverse (high to low) gift array on donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which gift array arrangement will produce the greatest donation rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2018 - 04/02/2018
Gift arrays have always been a point of interest for fundraisers. We’ve tested different amounts, different options, different minimums. We’ve even eliminated the array in favor of an open field. What we have not tested is the order in which to present the amounts.
Many nonprofits online, across all different verticals, when using a gift array choose to show the largest suggested amount first, rather than the lowest suggested amount, likely in hopes that donors will give more.
Does this matter? Especially for CaringBridge donors, who have been quite responsive in helping us understand gift arrays to-date.
How we test this: The control (original) keeps the standard rising suggested amount approach (start with the lowest ($50) on the left (assuming visitors in this case naturally read left to right) and then end with the highest ($250)
The treatment, however, reverses this, by starting with the highest amount first in the eyepath (in this case, left).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By showing the larger amount first, visitors were LESS likely to donate, and LESS likely to give in larger amounts.
But why?
When you think about the donation array, or suggested giving amounts, it all comes down to permission; meaning, what do we, as an organization, want the reader to believe is an ACCEPTABLE amount to give, and thus completely satisfy and receive that warm glow of giving?
By starting with a higher number first, we likely gave visitors the impression that lower amounts are LESS acceptable, despite them being on the list still. (for the same reason “other” is on the list… we won’t refuse their money, but maybe they will imagine us giving them a weird/disappointed look when they do)
Thus, because those visitors perceived their smaller donation was less acceptable, they chose not to donate at all.
Now, as for the drop in average gift approaching validity, this is likely due to the potential upgrade effect that happens when amounts are suggested in that rising order. By starting low, making the low amount feel acceptable, it is possible they feel like they can share a greater feeling of warmth by giving more, whereas in the opposite condition, giving just a little bit more doesn’t seem to mean any more than giving the original amount, due to its presentation.

experiments.title: How “need-based” copy with a quantified ask affects donor conversion during a matching challenge
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a quantified ask with a more straightforward appeal increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2017 - 03/23/2017
The treatment copy in the tribute widget produced a 30.1% increase in donor conversion—a significant increase in conversion. This suggests that CaringBridge hit on some powerful language that hits home with their donors, which inspired them to isolate how much the matching challenge affected results by re-testing this in a normal time period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had an area on their journals called the “tribute widget”. This widget asked the reader to honor their friend or loved one by making a donation in their honor. This had caused some clarity issues around who was receiving the gift, but testing and optimization had lowered the refund rate significantly.
They were entering a 48-hour matching challenge and wanted to test two new factors in the tribute widget. First, they wanted to make a more direct ask. Rather than asking the reader to “honor” their friend, they wanted to ask them to “help power [their] site”. Additionally, they wanted to include a quantified ask—giving the donor the handle of “$30 powers their site for one month”.
They set up an A/B test during the match period to see how it affected results.

experiments.title: How clarity affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does donor-centric language and a more personal connection increase the number of clicks compared to more succinct copy?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2014 - 11/25/2014
The treatment delivered 51.7% fewer clicks than the control. This says a lot about not only the audience viewing this, but the context in which the copy appears. It tells us that this audience values clarity over persuasion — a quick, short ask trumps a longer, more personal ask that attempts to draw in their emotions.
It also suggests that this page is highly functional – since it is a “dashboard” of sorts, people use it to get from one place to another, which suggests that future copy should be concise and to the point.
experiments.keyLearnings: CaringBridge had a donation ask on the homepage that registered users saw when they were logged in. We wanted to increase clickthroughs on this ask to generate more donations. We created a treatment that was highly donor-centric — it gave them a virtual “pat on the back” and reminded them of all the ways that they help those they care about.

experiments.title: How increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/31/2018
CaringBridge’s marketing team sought to increase the amount of users who got to their “ways to help” page, where there were several options to deepen engagement and provide more support to their friend or family member.
They thought that a slight copy change would align the “Ways to help” button on the journal page with the user’s intent. Instead of “Ways to help”, they proposed “Help [FirstName]” to encourage more people to click through. They thought this might work because they hypothesized that people who were reading this page were (consciously or unconsciously) desiring to help their friend or family member.
They were concerned about possible reactions to this test, so they only included 10% of site traffic in the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new copy produced a 19% lift in clickthrough rate for the “Ways to help” page—an impressive lift considering the small change. This points to more opportunities to align site copy with user intent throughout the site. With the volume of traffic that CaringBridge has, there is tremendous potential to increase clarity and adjust user experience accordingly.

experiments.title: How additional tactics improved giving on top of an already powerful match appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email will result in the greatest amount of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2018 - 04/02/2018
In past experiments, we have discovered a powerful motivating factor: the family story. And it makes sense: to the donor, it makes the benefit more concrete and it is presented in a way that human beings in general are best likely to absorb and process (story).
The lingering question, however, is to what end?  Is it possible to combine motivating factors like this family story with a match and to see an additive-like effect on total donations?
Especially for email?
experiments.keyLearnings:
At first glance, presenting the benefits of donating in the form of a story, and in a concrete example (clarity of value, construal theory), can have an additive motivating effect, even among match appeals where the motivation would presumably be high enough to begin with.

But that’s not all…

If you look at the sender, you have a CEO (less likely to be a genuine email) versus an actual CaringBridge User (more likely to be perceived genuine.

You also have more emphasis on a communal relationship rather than an exchange based relationship (i.e “your investment”).  Academic fundraising research has shown that major donors have been found to have more communal relationships with non-profits, and that communal strength and prosocial behavior is strongly correlated.

So it is possible that it was not just the story approach that helped propel the win, it was the combination of these other items as well:
Concrete vs. abstract
 	Sender line authenticity
 	Communal relationship language

experiments.title: How increasing the number of hyperlinks in an email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing the number of links in an email increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2016 - 01/16/2017
The treatment produced a 27% increase in clickthrough rate, which tells us a few things about our readers. First, it tells us that including more links incentivizes more of them to click, or take the first initial action at the moment that the value proposition hits home. Secondly, it tells us that we had previously been losing potential donors who didn’t make it all the way through the copy to the call-to-action.
This points to an important principle: our donors and prospects have varying levels of motivation. Some are ready to give and need very little convincing, while others are more deliberative and might need more copy to be incentivized to click. Typically, we attempt to address these varying levels of motivation through multiple emails, but this experiment points to the opportunity that we might be able to reach multiple levels of motivation in the same communication.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had always included a “focused link” in their email appeals, which gave readers a single link to click to take action. Historically, this had been a button, but previous testing had shown that a text link performed better.

They wanted to test the effect of giving donors more places to click throughout the email—essentially linking up any text that contained a CTA, so if the donor was motivated, they could take action right then. They produced a treatment that had the exact same copy, but included more clickable links.

				This experiment produced the following results:
27.1% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How additional value proposition copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the amount of value proposition copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2017 - 03/20/2017
CaringBridge sent a fundraising email to their housefile as part of a monthly appeal. The email told a personal story from a mother who had experienced the power of CaringBridge firsthand. Their control email told a compelling personal story using a convincing personal tone, but they thought it lacked the value proposition copy necessary to compel readers to give.
They created a treatment that increased the amount of copy significantly but provided (what they perceived to be) a stronger value proposition. They believed that even though this increased the time it took to read the email, that the additions would produce a greater return.
They split their file in half and launched an experiment to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 26.7% increase in donor conversion from the control, which showed that the amount of copy in the email is not as important as what it says. Not all words are created equal.

CaringBridge had done extensive testing in the area of personal tone—that is, getting the emails to read like an actual person wrote them. However, this concept needs to be balanced with the principle of value proposition. If the copy maintains a personal tone but does not convey enough value, then it’s just a nice communication piece.

This experiment is a good check against the concept of “long copy vs. short copy”—the most important concept is to get the right copy, regardless of length.

experiments.title: How isolating a quantified ask affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: When isolated, what impact does the quantified ask have on clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2017 - 03/27/2017
The first treatment, with just the quantified ask, produced a 26% increase in clickthrough rate. However, in further testing, it failed to produce any increase in conversion and produced a spike in refunds.
The second treatment, with the headline change only, produced a 10% decrease in clickthrough rate, which showed that the cumulative lift in previous testing had more correlation with the presence of the quantified ask, but was boosted by the headline as well.
This prompted further testing to see if they could clarify the message on the donation page to reduce confusion and refund rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge tested a radical redesign that produced a big lift in donor conversion. This was a valuable finding, but they wanted further clarity on the lift, since two factors were changed. In the first experiment, they changed the headline of their tribute widget and added a “quantified ask”, with the offer that a $30 gift would keep their friend or loved one’s site online for a month.

They were curious which element had the most impact between these two. So they created two treatments: one that reverted to the old headline and included the quantified ask, and one that reverted to the old body copy (without the quantified ask), but kept the winning headline.

They launched an A/B/C test to validate a winner and determine the impact of each element on conversion.
