The changes in this experience were to soften the headline “How are you?,” include an encouraging subhead, ghost text options in the modal itself instead of outside the experience, and update the CTA to “Share My Post.” It was large overhaul, but one where each of the elements were cohesively under the umbrella of making the modal itself more user-friendly and encouraging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the changes made to the journal entry modal did not have the intended effect of increasing journal posts. In fact the treatment resulted in a slight decrease, however, the statistical level of confidence is below the acceptable threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further analyze the user behavior and gather feedback to understand why the changes did not resonate with authors. This can help in crafting more effective interventions in the future. It's also important to continue testing different elements at various stages of the funnel to find the most impactful ways to increase journal posts.

experiments.title: How sending “View Full Post” traffic to the single-post page impacts tribute giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending “View Full Post” traffic to a single-post page will help improve the user-experience while also increasing traffic to the 16003 Tribute ask—increasing tribute giving and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2025 - 02/17/2025
In the current experience, longer posts from an author are shortened with a hyperlink “Show More” which then expands the full post. From a UI perspective, there are limitations to this experience. In mobile especially (where a majority of CB’s traffic originates from) the full post expanding in feed can overwhelm and disorient—requiring the user to scroll all the way back up or down to resume their browsing of the feed.
We hypothesize that by changing this hyperlinked copy to “View Full Post” and instead sending the traffic to the designated Single-Post Page will eliminate this friction, while also increasing Tribute giving. The single-post page contains a tribute widget (16003) that typically converts at a high volume. We believe this could accomplish both goals at once—making the experience more user-friendly while also sending more traffic to the 16003 Tribute ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To understand the key learnings from this experiment we must first differentiate two tracked metrics, that appear very similar at first glance. The first is Tribute Donations/Revenue (From Widget). This metric tracks tribute gifts only on the single-post page—with the 16003 code. This was important to analyze because ultimately it was a large part of our hypothesis: sending more traffic to single-post page will increase gifts to the widget on this page.

However, there is a more encompassing Tribute Gift metric that was measured in this test as well. Because users can technically make a tribute gift on an author's page to various different widgets as well as the donate button in navigation—tracking this metric was equally important. It would answer a key question: are we simply directing traffic to give on a different widget, or does this lead to an overall lift in gifts and revenue as well.

With this in mind, here are some key callouts from this experiment.
The click rate was about the same between "Show More" and "View Full Post." The changed copy did not necessarily mean users were more likely to expand the post.
 	There was a significant 1376.9% increase in donations to Tribute (Widget/16003) in the treatment with a 100% level of confidence. Confirming part of our hypothesis that by increasing traffic to the single-post page, we would increase donations to the 16003 Tribute Widget.
 	However, pulling back to analyze all Tribute Gifts and Revenue shows that the treatment seems to also result in a decrease overall. This is not statistically valid at an 80% level of confidence, however, the trend did remain consistent throughout the entirety of the test run. This could indicate something very important.
overall

experiments.title: How leading email appeal traffic to an aligned “holiday” donation page impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading email appeal traffic to an aligned “holiday appeal” donation page will increase donor conversion rate because it helps reinforce the 2-day match with additional value prop, while also tying the email into the larger December year-end goals.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, traffic from the email appeal is typically sent to a simplified form that is similar to the length of a general donation page – with match language added. We hypothesized that a longer, “holiday appeal” donation page that aligns with the overall campaign goals communicated in email and reinforces the 2-day match alongside the larger year-end goals will increase conversion rate
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that leading email appeal traffic to an aligned "holiday appeal" donation page can significantly increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 42.5% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the additional value proposition and alignment with the 2-day match and larger year-end goals had a positive impact on donor behavior.

However, the statistical level of confidence was 91.6%, which is just below the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for validity. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is needed for validity.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of leading email traffic to specialized donation pages tailored to specific campaigns or appeals. This experiment suggests that aligning the donation page with the messaging and goals of the email appeal can lead to increased donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How $100 Prefill vs a $100 Pre-Select in the Array Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $100 prefill for users on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because whereas a 3-option array seemed to introduce friction, an open $100 prefill accomplished the desired impact on average gift without introducing more options for the user to choose between..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2024 - 01/16/2025
On CaringBridge’s main donation page, $100 is the default pre-selected amount. However, how that number has been presented to the user has varied. The standard practice has been to have no gift array, but simply an open field with $100 prefilled. Alternatively, some pages utilized an array of three options with $100 as the preselected amount. It is worth noting that currently, neither of these options were present on the CaringBridge main donation page due to a migration glitch. We hypothesized that when testing between the two options, an open field with $100 prefilled (the way it was originally intended) would improve donor conversion over a 3-option array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

The treatment of using an open field with a $100 prefill on the main donation page resulted in a 35.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence was 92.0%, indicating a high likelihood that the increase in revenue was due to the treatment and not random chance. Furthermore, the hypothesis that a $100 prefill would improve donor conversion rates compared to a 3-option array was supported by the results of the experiment.

Ultimately, these results help enforce two next steps for action items. 1. That CaringBridge should consider testing their other donation pages which have a gift array by default, to see whether an open field pre-fill can also improve conversion rate. 2. CaringBridge should prioritize correcting the current main donation page so that the $100 prefill is represented, rather than the current blank open field (0 is the prefill)

experiments.title: How pinning a widget above a post on a landing page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pinning the tribute widget above a new author post for JEN traffic will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it increases visibility of the widget, but this reordering also better highlights the importance of the widget as a goal action for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2024 - 08/13/2024
When CaringBridge user makes a new post, their followers receive an email alert that takes them to directly the new journal entry on their profile. These users receive a Tribute Widget with an ask for a donation, however, this is pinned to be at the very bottom of the posts and any comments on the post in the control. If a particular post has many comments, this results in the Tribute Widget not being seen at all. We hypothesized that by pinning this tribute widget to be above the new post will resolve this potential point of friction and increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that pinning the tribute widget above a new author post for JEN traffic resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 24.3% increase in Donations - Tribute (from Widget) for All Traffic with a 91.2% statistical level of confidence.

The treatment also resulted in a directional increase in revenue as well, with an 18% lift and an 85% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the placement of the tribute widget and other elements on the platform to optimize donor conversion rates. It may also be valuable to explore additional factors, such as the timing of the donation ask in relation to the user's engagement with the content, to further improve the effectiveness of fundraising efforts on the platform. Additionally, considering the potential impact on user experience and engagement is crucial when making changes to the platform layout. By continuing to test and iterate based on data-driven insights, Caringbridge can better understand user behavior and preferences, ultimately leading to more successful fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How relocating the Tribute widget to the right for all users impacts tribute giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that relocating the tribute widget to the right side for visitors on a user’s profile will achieve an increase in tribute donations because it will spotlight the ask and keep the CTA’s distinct..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2024 - 06/11/2024
For CaringBridge visitors on a user profile, they are met with a few different action possibilities. They can read a User’s posts, leave a comment of their own, as well as a make a gift a through the Tribute Widget. Historically, this widget has been one of CaringBridge’s highest sources for donations on the site. With the migration to CB2 however, they received complaints from users who found the widget intrusive or misleading — implying that they must make a gift in order to leave a comment. This was not the case. To help address this confusion and remove potential friction for users who do not want to make a gift, we’ve hypothesized that pinning the Tribute Widget to the right would help better spotlight the ask and keep these two CTA’s (whether to comment or to make a gift) as separate for the visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that relocating the Tribute Widget to the right side for visitors on a user's profile did not achieve the intended increase in tribute donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

Before abandoning this experiment, we hypothesized a contributor that could be contributing to the decrease in Tribute Gifts:

Have we unintentionally reduced the visibility of the Tribute? There is potential to re-test with a widget that has higher visibility by outlining with a color or making the widget a solid color.

In future experimentation, it would be important to consider the potential impact of changing the placement of key elements on a website, particularly when it comes to donation prompts. It is crucial to test such changes thoroughly before implementing them site-wide. Additionally, further testing should be conducted to determine the actual impact of relocating the Tribute Widget and to understand why it may have led to a decrease in donations in this particular experiment.

experiments.title: How repositioning the tribute widget to be inside the comment section impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that unpinning the tribute widget from the top and placing it within the comment section for visitors on a user’s profile will achieve an increase in tribute gifts because it will address the perceived friction that the widget is a paywall.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
With the migration to  2.0, CaringBridge received complaints from users who found the tribute widget intrusive or misleading — implying that they must make a gift in order to leave a comment. This was not the case. To help address this confusion and remove potential friction for users who do not want to make a gift, we’ve hypothesized that pinning the Tribute Widget into the comment section rather than having it at the top would better spotlight the ask while also clearing up the misconception that there was a paywall to commeting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that moving the tribute widget from the top of the page to within the comment section did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing tribute gifts. In fact, there was a 21.2% decrease in donations for all traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group. While the statistical level of confidence was only 93.5%, indicating that the results are not yet fully valid, they are approaching validity.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the placement and design of features such as the tribute widget to ensure they do not create confusion or friction for users. It may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different variations or to gather qualitative feedback from users to better understand their perceptions and preferences. Additionally, it is essential to monitor user behavior and donation patterns closely to accurately assess the impact of any changes made.

experiments.title: How increasing an ask from $30 to $50 impacts giving for onsite tribute widgets
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the ask amount  for site visitors will achieve an increase in revenue  because there is a larger initial ask (without doubling the previous amount).
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/12/2024
CaringBridge has a long-standing ask of $30 on their on-site tribute widgets. This was the approximate cost to power a site for a month several years ago but has since needed updating. In order to get closer to the actual cost to-date, we tested using a $50 ask instead – knowing that we could potential lose donors but perhaps make up the difference in revenue by seeing a higher average gift. This was tested among multiple places on-site where a tribute widget appeared.
The first test ran (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/j6kp) was a $60 ask and that proved to be too high for site visitors – resulting in a valid 16% decrease in conversion rate and a decrease in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Increasing the ask amount for site visitors from $30 to $50 resulted in a 10.3% decrease (LoC 90%) in donations for all traffic. When looking at revenue we saw a 1.7% decrease (LoC 40%) - which is virtually no difference in revenue. While the larger ask decreased conversion rate, it did not decrease conversion rate as much as the $60 ask and the higher average gift made up for the difference in revenue.
It's suggested that in the next iteration of the test, we pose a lower ask for a shorter time period (i.e. $25 powers a site for 2 weeks) in order lower the barrier to entry and increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding a Security Callout to a Donation Page Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the security of an online gift with a callout line for prospective tribute donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because eliminate a potential point of friction to giving — mainly the suspicion that sharing payment info or making an online gift isn’t secure.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 12/26/2023
CaringBridge’s Tribute Donation Page already has elements to reinforce security around the giving experience: a shaded box around the payment info and a lock icon. However, we hypothesized that an added line of copy in the payment section, “Your Information is Secure” would help tie together these elements, call out the security of an online gift, and increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a line of copy to reinforce the security of an online gift in the payment section of the Tribute Donation Page resulted in an 8.2% increase in donations. Although the results are not statistically valid, they are approaching validity with a 87.5% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a follow-up experiment with a larger sample size or longer duration to increase the level of confidence and validate the results.

Overall, the findings of this experiment suggest that calling out the security of the giving experience can help reduce any suspicions or concerns potential donors may have about sharing payment information. By addressing this potential point of friction, organizations like CaringBridge can potentially increase conversion rates and encourage more online donations.

experiments.title: How aligning call-to-action verbs in a tribute widget impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning all the call-to-action words in the triibue widget for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because there will be additional clarity and affirmation in the conversion process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/10/2023
CaringBridge’s users heavily rely on the tribute widget in order to make a gift in honor of their loved one. In order to increase conversion rate, we sought to add clarity to the pre-existing copy by using consistent CTA’s and driving the desired action: give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning all the call-to-action words in the tribute widget did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, there was a decrease of 9.3% in donations for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid.

Additionally, we saw the results vacillating between flat and a slight decrease throughout the experiment. This suggests that creating congruency in the call-to-action words for the tribute widget is not a significant motivating factor for journal page viewers. They likely already have enough clarity from the existing copy about what the tribute widget is asking of them

It's suggested we test other concepts in the copy such as an emotional appeal to see if that will drive an increase in giving.

experiments.title: How aligning call-to-action verbs in a tribute widget impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning all the call-to-action words in the triibue widget for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because there will be additional clarity and affirmation in the conversion process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/10/2023
CaringBridge’s users heavily rely on the tribute widget in order to make a gift in honor of their loved one. In order to increase conversion rate, we sought to add clarity to the pre-existing copy by using consistent CTA’s and driving the desired action: give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning all the call-to-action words in the tribute widget did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, there was a decrease of 9.3% in donations for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid.

Additionally, we saw the results vacillating between flat and a slight decrease throughout the experiment. This suggests that creating congruency in the call-to-action words for the tribute widget is not a significant motivating factor for journal page viewers. They likely already have enough clarity from the existing copy about what the tribute widget is asking of them

It's suggested we test other concepts in the copy such as an emotional appeal to see if that will drive an increase in giving.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate (Pt.3)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prompting people to create a “page” or “journal” instead of a “site” will increase site creation rate because there will be a reduced perception of work involved which will reduce anxiety as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
When we ran the first iteration of this test we ran the experiment on one offer. We saw a valid lift when using “page.” Before rolling it out to all offers, we decided to test it among all offers and add another variant using “journal” to see if that would also reduce anxiety. In the second experiment, we saw a decrease in conversion rate when using “page.”
In this particular experiment we tested this concept on a separate offer that used slightly different verbiage in the button and started with the language “page” under the first assumption that people responded more to the use of “page”. Therefore, the control is “page,” treatment 1 is “site,” and treatment 2 is “journal.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment affirms what was uncovered in experiment #2 - "page" (the control in this experiment) is not universally beneficial. In fact the usage of "site" resulted in a 93% increase in site creation with a level of confidence of 80%. While the use of "journal" resulted in a 138% increase in site creation with a level of confidence of 91%.
Based on this, it would be beneficial to continue testing the use of "journal" as a potential alternative to "site" or "page".
Overall, these findings suggest that there is value in exploring alternative language options to reduce perceived commitment or anxiety when prompting users to create a site on CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How a slide-out with value prop affected Co-author sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prominently featuring a slide-out for CaringBridge users without a Co-author will achieve an increase in Co-Author shares because the slide-out not only educates on the value, it makes it easier to share..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2023 - 05/21/2023
CaringBridge allows users to share their page access with a friend or family member—making them a Co-Author on their CaringBridge page. We hypothesized that making this a more prominent feature on the user’s homepage, would not only help inform them what a Co-Author is—but would also increase the sign-up/share rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main finding of the experiment is that featuring a slide-out for CaringBridge users without a Co-author led to a statistically significant increase in the rate of sending invitations to co-authors.
However, the level of confidence suggests that further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of the slide-out feature in promoting co-author shares and to identify the conditions and segments where it works best. Future experiments could focus on refining the design of the slide-out or testing different formats or placements to assess their impact on co-author shares.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a landing page impacts site creation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimony for prospective site creators will achieve an increase in site creation conversion rate because social proof reduces anxiety and adds credibility to the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2023 - 04/11/2023
In oder to increase the conversion rate for site creations, we ran a test where we added social proof in the form of a testimonial on a landing page.
This test was ran on an ebook funnel where the confirmation page for their ebook had a call to action to create a site with CaringBridge. The control for this experiment omitted any kind of testimonial whereas the treatment included a real-life quote from someone who has benefited from created a site. Our hypothesis that adding social proof will reduce any kind of anxiety someone may feel when creating a site with CaringBridge. This quote adds credibility to those who may be unfamiliar with CaringBridge.
This was tested on one ebook funnel and traffic was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a testimonial to a confirmation page may not have the intended effect of reducing anxiety and adding credibility to an organization. The treatment resulted in a 51.0% directional decrease in website creation compared to the control, but the statistical level of confidence was only 86.6%. This means that the results are not considered valid but are approaching validity.
These results are contrary to what was expected. It's probable that the selected testimonial may not be relevant enough to the subject matter of the ebook for this funnel. It's recommended that this is retested in a different ebook funnel to draw further conclusions. Additionally, CaringBridge could anchor the benefits of creating a site by using language like "Join over XXX,XXX families who have benefited from using CaringBridge". This reference point suggests that there are many others who have used CaringBridge successfully, which may add credibility and increase the perceived value of using the platform.

experiments.title: How communal language affects donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 12/20/2020
CaringBridge was running a matching gift campaign for two days as part of their holiday appeal. They had previously tested a sticky bar during a previous match that employed “communal” language (as opposed to transactional language) that increased both clickthrough rate and conversion rate during the match. They wanted to extend that language to their tribute donation pages, where people can give in honor of a specific journal author.
The original page positioned CaringBridge as the hero—it helped the author stay connected to family and friends. Then, it positioned the reader as the one who makes that happen. Instead, they decided to double down on language that addressed the prospective donor as the hero, removing CaringBridge entirely. First, they changed the headline to thank the donor for directly helping the author stay connected to their loved ones. Then, they reinforced that (and introduced CaringBridge) by adding exclusivity to the subheadline.
Finally, they applied the communal language theory to the call-to-action that mentioned the match. The original CTA offered a “dollar for dollar” match if the donor gave—which appeared to be transactional language. It also brought the brand “CaringBridge” back into the conversation. They removed the brand name from the CTA and provided a “pressure release valve” that put the power of whether to participate or not into the hands of the donor. Finally, they removed the “dollar for dollar” language and launched a test to see if their hypothesis was proven correct.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language did not appear to have a significant impact overall. However, when the team dug into the device types, they found that desktop visitors—which made up the majority of donations—showed a 20.2% increase in conversion rate. On top of this increase in conversion rate, desktop visitors showed a 29.6% increase in revenue with a 98% level of confidence. Not only did more people give—they gave larger amounts!

This showed the potential impact of replacing transactional language with communal language. Next, the CaringBridge team wanted to re-test this language in a non-match scenario to see if it would have year-round impact.

experiments.title: How a personalized sticky bar on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalizing the sticky bar on the donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2019 - 06/08/2019
CaringBridge had run experiments to show that inserting a “sticky bar” at the top of donation pages could increase conversion rate during one of their matching gift appeal. However, they had recently updated their site code to be able to personalize the messages on the sticky bar to match the person’s donation page that the bar was running on. They wanted to test the impact of this during a match appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The personalized version of the sticky bar increased conversion by 10.1%, though it did not reach full statistical validity because it only ran for two days of the match period. This is a good directional learning, though it is muddied by another result—when this test was re-run later in the second match, it decreased conversion by 9.7%, also not statistically valid.
Ultimately, this might be too small of a change to validate a consistent result. However, the sticky bar has continued to generate increases in conversions across the board, especially on mobile.

experiments.title: How visual reinforcement on the donation page increases mobile conversion during a match
experiments.researchQuestion: How will visual reinforcement affect donor conversion on the donation page during a short-term matching gift appeal?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/20/2019
CaringBridge was running a Valentine’s Day fundraising campaign and had a $25,000 matching gift. They knew that most of their donations on these short, high-urgency appeals came through a few donation funnels that landed on the same page. While previous tests had focused on driving traffic, they wanted to see if they could optimize conversion by driving home the match language on the two days that the gift was applied. They created two versions of a sticky top banner for the donation page—one that emphasized the match for “your” gift, and another that emphasized the match for “all donations”. They launched a three-way experiment split between mobile and desktop to see how it affected conversions on mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: The personal match language expressed on the sticky bar increased conversion by 16.5%. Though this only reached a 90.1% level of confidence (and is not valid) it was close enough  (especially when combined with the desktop results) to show that this visual reinforcement is a powerful tool to provide extra motivation through the donation process—even if it is only one step.

experiments.title: How refreshing imagery affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an image more closely tied to the mission increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2015 - 11/12/2015
CaringBridge wanted to increase conversion on their tribute donation pages by adding imagery that resonated with their mission of connecting people during life’s most difficult times. They tested an image of clasped hands to see if it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This was a fascinating test, as a simple image change produced a significant result. This test inspired a new test to see if simply changing the image randomly (which can produce novelty bias) can produce a similar (or greater) lift.

experiments.title: How personalization in donation page headline copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personalized, donor-centric headline outperform a clear, direct headline?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 04/11/2016
Previous CaringBridge tests had shown that in the tribute funnel (where visitors give in honor of their friend of loved one), personalization improved the likelihood of donor conversion. However, previous experiments had also shown that a clear, direct headline (in this case “Give to CaringBridge”) resulted in the highest donor conversion.
The team wanted to test whether a personalized, donor-centric call to action, though longer and less direct, would improve the clarity of the value proposition, present a stronger incentive, and result in higher donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment showed that visitors’ likelihood to convert increased significantly when they were reminded of the impact of their gift. With CaringBridge copy, there has always been a balance between clarity and personalization. Through this tribute funnel, where the gift is closely tied to a personal relationship, personalization wins.

experiments.title: How organization-centric language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing organizational-centric language increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2016 - 05/05/2016
CaringBridge had performed several successful experiments to personalize the copy on their “tribute” donation pages. But user feedback had shown one interesting organizational fact: most people didn’t know CaringBridge was a nonprofit organization. This had produced confusion before, so they developed a single sentence of copy to add to the page in pursuit of clarity:
“Nearly 90 percent of our funding comes from people like you who have experienced the power of CaringBridge firsthand.”
This copy was never tested, and we developed a hypothesis that it might be what we call “organizational-centric” language. This type of language is not inherently bad, but it might clash with the tribute page, which is highly personal.
We developed a treatment that eliminated this sentence and set out to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (without the sentence) increased conversion by 6.6% — not an overwhelming amount, but enough to show that taking the donor’s attention away from the person impacted by the gift reduced the likelihood that they would give.
This doesn’t solve the marketing problem that many people don’t know CaringBridge is a nonprofit, but it tells us that the donation page is not the place to communicate that.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button link to non-tribute affects donor conversion rate on site profiles
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button links to be the non-tribute page for site profile visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  the donate buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept, would lead to potential confusion and friction about the message. Therefore, leading the users to the non-tribute page that had more missional language, would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2026 - 01/27/2026
On site profiles, all donate buttons usually lead to the tribute donation page for CaringBridge.
For this test, we wanted to change the donate buttons in the header, middle of the page and the footer to lead to the non-tribute donation page instead of the tribute page. The tribute widget would still lead to the tribute donation page, maintaining that continuity.
This was tested on site profiles and was tested on both mobile and desktop. This was tested when there were no campaigns or matches going on for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Changing the donate button links to the non-tribute page for site profile visitors led to a 7.8% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. This indicates that the change had a positive impact on donor conversion rate, potentially reducing confusion and friction for users.
In future experimentation, it is important to note that while the results of this experiment suggest a positive impact on donor conversion rate, the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended 95%. Therefore, these results should be interpreted with caution and not used for making definitive decisions.
However, the insights gained from this experiment can guide future testing strategies. It is worth exploring further variations in the donation button links to see if similar positive results can be achieved with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, considering the significant increases in both donations and revenue from the non-tribute donation page, incorporating more missional language and context into donation pages could be a valuable strategy to enhance donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button link to non-tribute affects donor conversion rate on site profiles
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button links to point to the non-tribute page for site profile visitors will increase the donor conversion rate because the donate buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept could lead to potential confusion and friction with the message. Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate. Donation buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept could lead to potential confusion and friction with the message. Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
On site profiles, all donate buttons usually lead to the tribute donation page for CaringBridge.
For this test, we wanted to change the donate buttons in the header, middle of the page, and the footer to lead to the non-tribute donation page instead of the tribute page. The tribute widget would still lead to the tribute donation page, maintaining that continuity.
This was tested on site profiles and on both mobile and desktop. This was tested when there were no campaigns or matches going on for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the donation buttons on site profiles from leading to the tribute donation page to the non-tribute donation page resulted in an 8.4% decrease in donations across all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experiments, it is important to ensure the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider the timing of the experiment, such as avoiding conducting tests during peak donation periods like the Calendar Year End campaign. It's also important to carefully monitor and analyze the impact of any changes on donation metrics before making broader implementations.

experiments.title: How an exit intent with a thermometer and an exit intent with social proof affects donor conversion rate during Calendar Year End
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because for the donor goal thermometer version, the image would highlight what the user could do to fill the gap, and the social proof image would show how the user could make an impact along with the other people that have already made an impact, encouraging the user to help CB meet the match.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
During their 2-day matches, CaringBridge usually has an exit intent that appears on-site profiles, featuring language that reflects the urgency of the match and how the user can help CaringBridge reach its goal.
During this particular match at the end of the calendar year, we wanted to try an A/B/C test with different exit intents that used varying visuals on the exit intent.
The first treatment, which had an exit intent, featured a thermometer image, showing how the user could help fill the gap.
The second treatment, exit intent, featured an image of people, highlighting how they could help reach the goal among the community of people who had already contributed.
This was tested on both mobile and desktop devices, as well as on-site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rate. The treatment showed an 8.2% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the visuals used in the exit intent may have had a positive impact on user engagement.

There was an increase in revenue for both mobile and desktop, which indicated that the social proof image variant was a clear winner. For desktop, there was a 19.37% increase in revenue. For mobile, there was a 24% increase in revenue.

Control (desktop) - $7,208

Treatment 1(desktop) - $7,515

Treatment 2 (desktop) - $8,770

Control (mobile) - $9,686

Treatment 1 (mobile) - $10,665

Treatment 2 (mobile) - $12,200

So the conclusion for this test is that while both variants produced an almost similar amount of donations, the variant with the social proof image encouraged users to give higher gifts, resulting in an overall increase in revenue.

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact statement below the donate button affects transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic impact statement  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in transactions because the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission. because  the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2025 - 12/02/2025
During Giving Tuesday, we wanted to test adding a dynamic impact statement below the donate button for CaringBridge on their donation pages. The statement pulled the number of donors on the donation page during the day of Giving Tuesday. The statement read as follows –
Your gift’s impact will combine with the X other donors who have also stepped forward to DOUBLE their Giving Tuesday gift alongside the $50,000 match!
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic impact statement on the donation page did not have a significant positive impact on transactions during Giving Tuesday. In fact, the treatment group saw a 3.9% decrease in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 31.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.
This suggests that the dynamic impact statement did not effectively encourage visitors to make a donation based on the number of donors who had already contributed. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and placement of social proof elements on donation pages to ensure that they resonate with visitors and positively impact their decision to donate.
It is also essential to conduct experiments for a longer duration to gather sufficient data and increase the confidence level of the results before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the findings. Additionally, considering different types of social proof elements or adjusting the messaging based on visitor behavior and preferences could be explored in future experiments to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How a double impact slideup affects transactions during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a double impact slide-up for homepage visitors will increase transactions because it highlights the urgency and potential impact the visitor could have, encouraging them to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2025 - 12/03/2025
We have seen that slide-ups have been very successful for CaringBridge during high-urgency campaigns. For Giving Tuesday, we wanted to test a regular slide-up vs a slide-up that highlighted the chance for the user to have their gift doubled.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a double impact slide-up for homepage visitors did not yield the expected increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 45.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making. In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the experiment's duration to ensure a sufficient sample size and sufficient time for the impact of the changes to be accurately measured. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the double impact message or explore other ways to create urgency and highlight the potential impact of donations to see if different strategies yield better results. It is also important to consider the overall messaging and timing of the campaign to ensure it resonates with the target audience.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky stacked navigation bar with a “Donate Monthly” button affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “donate monthly” button on a stacked navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in recurring donations because having a separate button would draw more attention to it, resulting in users clicking on the button and making a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2025
For this experiment, we added a sticky stacked navigation bar for mobile, but we also added a “Donate Monthly” button to see how that would affect transactions. We had previously tested this with just a donate button, and we had seen that there was an increase in clicks to the donate button when in a stacked navigation bar. This experiment was run for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a "donate monthly" button to a stacked navigation bar for mobile users can potentially increase donations. The treatment group saw a significant increase in donations, indicating that having a separate button for monthly donations could draw more attention and encourage users to donate regularly.

However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 95%, which means that these results should not be considered reliable for future decision-making.

We did see an increase in clicks to "donate monthly button". We saw a valid increase in clicks.

But ultimately, we saw a decrease in overall transactions, therefore indicating that this had a negative effect on transactions. We saw a 6.3% decrease in transactions. This was not a valid decrease in transactions.

For future experimentation, it is essential to run further tests with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the impact of adding a "donate monthly" button. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence donation behavior on mobile devices, such as the placement of the button, messaging, and design. Additionally, conducting usability testing and gathering user feedback can provide valuable insights for optimizing the donation process on mobile platforms.

experiments.title: How an animated heart and monthly callout impacts giving on Non-Tribute donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated heart and monthly callout for users on the non-tribute donation page will achieve an increase in monthly giving because it draws attention the monthly option, while preserving the donor’s autonomy, then after being clicked reinforces the decision with an impact callout.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 05/16/2025
On CaringBridge’s main donation page (Non-Tribute), we tested an animated heart over the monthly tab, which, once clicked, would display callout copy reinforcing the impact of a recurring gift. We hypothesize that drawing attention to monthly giving, while still preserving the donor’s autonomy, will increase monthly giving. In this experiment, the monthly tab is not pre-selected and the callout is only shown after a user clicks the monthly tab—breaking the steps into two distinct actions: first encouraging the donor to select this tab, then reinforcing the action they’ve taken.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which included an animated heart and a monthly callout on the non-tribute donation page, did not lead to an increase in monthly giving. In fact, there was a 15.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 72.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

It is worth noting that this same experience was conducted on both the Non-Tribute donation page as well as CaringBridge's Tribute donation page. In the latter, this experience did not increase monthly giving, but it did result in greater Tribute gifts overall, whereas here the same experience has decreased transactions overall.

This encourages further consideration around how the two donation pages are different, the diverging motivations of traffic on both to where the same experience could have a reverse effect depending on the page.

experiments.title: How does setting the comment box to “Active” impact visitor comments on posts?

rows.name: Focus Comment Box when View All clicked

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting the comment box to “Active” for visitors, after they click “View All Comments” will achieve an increase in comments because it eliminates an extra step and point of friction—allowing the user to begin typing immediately..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/09/2025 - 07/11/2025
A key metric for CaringBridge is increasing Visitor comments on an Author’s post. In the current logic, when a user clicks “View All Comments” on a user’s post, they still have to click into the comment box again to make it “Active,” ensuring they can begin typing and post a reply. We hypothesize that eliminating this extra step, updating the comment box so that a Visitor can immediately begin typing after they click “View All” will increase comments.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved setting the comment box to "Active" for visitors after they click "View All Comments," resulted in a 1.6% decrease in comments for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 69.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid, and the experiment did not provide conclusive evidence to support the hypothesis. The primary takeaway from this experiment is that it made no difference to the status quo.

experiments.title: How increasing specificity and personalization on the Tribute donate button affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that treatment copy for the call-to-action on the Tribute donate button will achieve an increase in conversions because the two options leverage either greater specificity around the kind of a gift a user is making, or personalizes with who they’re making the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2025 - 07/17/2025
On CaringBridge’s Tribute donation page, the Donate button’s CTA was simply: Submit Secure Donation. We developed two treatments to improve the conversion rate on this page that targeted this final call-to-action. The first, hypothesized that greater specificity: Submit My Tribute Gift would increase donor conversion—better reflecting the kind of gift the user was making. The second iteration, tested personalization of the CTA button. A tribute gift is a donation to CaringBridge made in honor of a particular person. We believe that displaying that person’s name in the donate button itself would increase conversion rate—reiterating who the tribute was being made to.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which aimed to increase conversions by either providing greater specificity in the call-to-action or personalizing the donation button, did not result in a statistically significant increase in donations. In fact, there was a 1.4% decrease in Tribute Donations with a 19.6% level of confidence. This means that the control was more effective in this case. Additionally, it's worth noting that both treatments resulted in a decrease (with statistical validity) in the average gift versus the control donate button.

experiments.title: How changing the copy above the comment box affects comments
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy above the comment box for profile visitors will achieve an increase in comments on posts because the copy options encourage users to leave a comment by describing how it would positively affect the user who posted.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2025 - 07/07/2025
CaringBridge has a feature that allows users to leave comments on posts. There is a line of copy that encourages users to leave a comment on a post. For this test, we wanted to test how changing that copy option would affect users leaving comments on posts. This was tested on site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy above the comment box on site profiles resulted in a small increase in comments left for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 46.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is a sufficient sample size and statistical significance before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the copy to see if there is a stronger impact on user behavior. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence user engagement, such as the design of the comment box, the visibility of the call-to-action, or the overall user experience.

experiments.title: How changing the color of the call to action button on tribute widgets affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the tribute widget call to action color for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donor conversion rate because changing the color would provide more visibility to the button and provide more clarity as to what the button is for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2025 - 07/07/2025
For CaringBridge, their website primarily contains their purple brand color. That is the case for the call to action buttons for the tribute widgets as well. That can potentially cause the button to be blended in with the other elements on the website and not be entirely visible for users. For this test, we wanted to test how changing the button to blue would affect tribute donations. This was tested on all of site tribute widgets.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Changing the tribute widget call to action color from purple to blue resulted in a 2.8% increase in Donations - Tribute for All Traffic, but with a low statistical confidence level of 38.3%. This indicates that the change may have had a positive impact on donor conversion, but the results are  inconclusive.
 	However, Revenue - Tribute experienced a significant 19% decrease with a high level of confidence (100%). This indicates that while the number of donations may have increased, the total revenue generated from tributes decreased.
The impact of changes on multiple metrics: While an increase in donations is positive, it is essential to also consider the impact on overall revenue. It's important to strike a balance between increasing donations and maintaining or increasing revenue.
 	Statistical confidence level: Results with a low statistical confidence level like the one in this experiment should not be relied upon for decision-making. It's crucial to ensure that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant to make informed decisions.
 	Further testing: To validate the impact of changing the tribute widget call to action color, additional testing with a larger sample size and longer duration may be necessary. This will help in drawing more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How moving the tribute widget in the Updates Tab affects tribute donations

rows.name: Relocate Tribute Widget in Updates Tab

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the tribute widget in the updates tab for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donations because moving the tribute widget would reduce the confusion of the widget appearing as a paywall before the posts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2025 - 06/17/2025
For this CaringBridge experiment, we wanted to move the tribute widget in the updates tab to be below the comments to see how that would affect tribute donations. This was similar to an experiment we had done for the single post page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the tribute widget in the updates tab below the comments resulted in a 21.6% increase in Donations - Tribute for All Traffic. This indicates that the change had a positive impact on donation conversions.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it might be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data and improve the statistical validity of the findings.

Overall, while the initial results suggest that moving the tribute widget may have a positive effect on donations, further testing is needed to confirm these findings. It is important to approach experimentation with a careful and analytical mindset, considering both statistical significance and practical significance when evaluating the results.

experiments.title: How adding in more copy on a recurring donation page affects recurring donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in more copy for recurring donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the copy will provide some clarity about why they should donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2025 - 03/27/2025
For this test, we tested adding in some more copy on the monthly donation page as a way to provide more clarity and more value proposition to the user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding more copy to the recurring donation page did not have a positive impact on donation conversions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level below 95%. Therefore, we cannot draw reliable conclusions from these findings.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue testing hypotheses and variations on donation pages, but with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure more valid and reliable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider qualitative feedback from users to understand their preferences and motivations when it comes to donating.

experiments.title: How rotating a “Gift Card” widget with a Tribute Thank-You message impacts clicks and tributes
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rotating a Gift Card widget in with the tribute Thank You card for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in Gift Card clicks/traffic without decreasing tribute giving because the additional CTA is only shown to those who’ve already donated, providing them with an additional next step rather than detracting from another important CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 03/10/2025
This experiment was a development upon a previous test to promote Gift Cards/Ways to Help as an additional CTA on user profiles. In the original test, the Gift Card widget had been swapped in place of the Tribute Widget on a rotating logic. Although this did dramatically increase clicks and traffic to the gift card page, it also resulted in a valid 30% decrease in tribute giving.
In this iteration, we hypothesized that only rotating the Gift Card in with the Tribute thank-you message would ensure that the additional CTA could be communicated, without removing another important ask, but instead, prioritizing the next step for people who’ve already donated. The logic for this setup was 50/50, half the time users would see the thank-you card for their tribute gift and the other half of the time they’d receive the additional Gift Card widget. Versus in the control, they were only shown the thank you message.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Rotating a Gift Card widget in with the tribute Thank You card for users on a site profile resulted in a 17.5% increase in Send a Gift Card clicked for All Traffic. This indicates that the additional CTA was effective in generating more clicks and traffic to the gift card page.

2. The 78.1% statistical level of confidence is below the 95% threshold, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid. This suggests that the increase in Gift Card clicks may not be entirely reliable.

3. However, another key aspect of this test was to incorporate the Gift Card widget without negatively impacting tribute giving. In this case, the treatment was a success. Reversing the original decrease seen previously, while also indicating that this treatment can be safely shown and likely will result in great education and clicks to Gift Cards.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the importance of careful consideration and testing when introducing new elements to a site profile. By analyzing the results and refining future experiments based on these findings, we can improve the user experience and drive desired actions effectively.

experiments.title: How a consistent CTA throughout the donor funnel impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a consistent CTA for the housefile during a high-urgency appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it helps reinforce one clear CTA with consistency, rather than mixing calls to action. because it helps reinforce one clear CTA with consistency, rather than mixing calls to action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that a consistent CTA from the email to donation page copy and final “donate/give” button would help reinforce the desired action and increased donor conversion rate. In the control, the ask was presented with various ways of phrasing the call to action: “make a gift” “donate” “give” “match your donation” etc. In the treatment, we used “donate” consistently as the CTA in email copy and donation page copy while also changing the give button to “Donate.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a consistent call-to-action (CTA) does not always lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this case, using "donate" consistently as the CTA in both the email and donation page did not result in a higher number of donations compared to using various CTAs like "make a gift" or "match your donation."

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be worthwhile to test these in other funnels – besides just email (consistent CTA from a Sticky Bar or Facebook ad for example), in addition to swapping the consistency for a different CTA (using Give throughout rather than Donate throughout)

experiments.title: How an identity call to action in email, emphasizing “become a donor,” impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an identity CTA for non-donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it helps emphasize the user becoming a donor, speaking to their identity/what they’re joining, versus the action CTA of “make a gift”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that using an identity CTA for non-donors, emphasizing “become a donor” rather than just “make a gift” will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The hypothesis that using an identity CTA for non-donors emphasizing "become a donor" would increase donor conversion rate was not supported by the results.

2. The treatment group, which used the identity CTA, actually saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

3. The statistical confidence level of 66.4% is below the standard threshold of 95%, meaning that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It is possible that for non-donors, the identity CTA framed the ask in a way that felt too big. As someone whose never given, "become a donor" may seem more intimidating than "make a gift" having a reverse effect given our hypothesis. It would be worthwhile to re-test this experience for higher validity during a period outside of the increased urgency.

experiments.title: How impacted gift array links in email impacts donor conversion during a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because it eliminates friction, pre-filling the selected amount on the donation page, while also reinforcing that their gift is being matched in the copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, we hypothesized that using impacted gift array links to drive urgency and eliminate friction during the 2-day match would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign led to a decrease in donations for all traffic. Additionally, the treatment saw a dropoff in average gift with $146 in the control versus $65 in the treatment.

Ultimately, the level of confidence was 78.9%, meaning these results are not valid for future decision-making. It would also be worth re-testing this experience for validity outside the window of a high-urgency appeal. Potentially, the treatment may have resulted in too much decision friction at the top of the funnel, whereas the links would have proved more effective simply by increasing clicks and greater traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a standard overlay compares to an overlay with a gift array during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array for site traffic during the final day of the holiday appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because the array prefills the selected amount onto the donation page, eliminating friction and providing users who are likely coming to the site in order to make their gift with a first step on page load.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2024 - 01/01/2025
During the final day of CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, half the traffic received a standard urgency overlay whereas the other half received an overlay with an interactive gift array. The frequency and amount the user selected would then be prefilled onto the donation page, eliminating friction and helping to encourage a higher gift amount. We hypothesized that the treatment would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue, providing users with a gift array on page load – assuming that much of the traffic will be coming to CaringBridge.org on the final day of the year in order to make their year-end gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array, resulted in a 5.3% increase in donations for all traffic on the final day of the holiday campaign. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 48.8%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.

It's important to note that this experiment was conducted during the final day of the year, where urgency is extremely high and uniquely motivated. As seen in the little to no difference between control and treatment, it is possible that users on the site at this time already know that they intend to make their gift – meaning that whether encountering version 1 or version 2 it doesn't significantly sway that decision negatively or positively.

experiments.title: How a Yes/No Slideout Compares to a Two-Step Slideout
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a two-step slideout for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it provides the reader more autonomy answering the first question, and a clearer understanding of where the CTA will take them in the second.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2024 - 12/29/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that a Yes/No slideout broken into two steps would increase donor conversion rate from homepage traffic. In the control, readers are presented with a Yes/No slideout, in which answering “yes” to the question of value then takes the user to the donation page. We believed that by breaking this question into a two-step process: “Have you been impacted by CaringBridge” and if so, “Will you make a gift?” will afford the reader more autonomy, while also better priming them for the main question of whether or not they will give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the two-step slideout for users on the homepage did not achieve the increased donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations for all traffic compared to the control group. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 18.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Based on these results, it is not advisable to make any significant changes to the donation process on the CaringBridge homepage. Future experimentation should focus on different variables or approaches to improving donor conversion rates. It is essential to carefully analyze the results of each experiment and ensure that the statistical significance is robust before making any decisions based on the findings.

experiments.title: How campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page impacts revenue during a holiday appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that campaign-related copy for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the value prop is expanded upon, greater urgency is tied to the campaign, and the donor has a sense of how their gift fits into the December goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, the Tribute Donation page largely remains the same. Aside from brief mentions of the match, the copy is stripped down, short, and doesn’t tie into the larger goals of the organization – instead focusing more on a donor’s gift to the individual. We believed that by using the campaign donation page on Tribute, tying to the larger December goals and reinforcing the match more, it would increase average gift and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page led to a 3.6% increase in revenue for all traffic as well as a slight bump in donations overall. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.7%, below the 85% threshold for validity.

However, it is an encouraging first step to continue testing on the Tribute donation page, which historically, has been treated very separate from the rest of CaringBridge's campaign and goals – because users are there for a specific user. To have this broadened and more general appeal copy not result in a decrease, but also perhaps have a slight positive increase, could open up door for continued testing.

experiments.title: How campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page impacts revenue during a holiday appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that campaign-related copy for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the value prop is expanded upon, greater urgency is tied to the campaign, and the donor has a sense of how their gift fits into the December goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, the Tribute Donation page largely remains the same. Aside from brief mentions of the match, the copy is stripped down, short, and doesn’t tie into the larger goals of the organization – instead focusing more on a donor’s gift to the individual. We believed that by using the campaign donation page on Tribute, tying to the larger December goals and reinforcing the match more, it would increase average gift and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page led to a 3.6% increase in revenue for all traffic as well as a slight bump in donations overall. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.7%, below the 85% threshold for validity.

However, it is an encouraging first step to continue testing on the Tribute donation page, which historically, has been treated very separate from the rest of CaringBridge's campaign and goals – because users are there for a specific user. To have this broadened and more general appeal copy not result in a decrease, but also perhaps have a slight positive increase, could open up door for continued testing.

experiments.title: How a Slide-Up performs against an Exit-Intent during a Holiday Appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-up with gift array for users on the CaringBridge site will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because the experience is less intrusive than the pop-up, and the in-line array pre-fills the donor’s selected amount on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we conducted a re-test of the Slide-Up with gift array vs the standard Exit Intent pop-up. The goal was to determine whether there was a significant impact upon donations and revenue during a high urgency appeal – where motivation is significantly higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-up with gift array did not perform as well as the standard exit intent pop-up in terms of donations during the high urgency appeal. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 55.0%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.

However, this test did help troubleshoot an earlier discrepancy between the slide-up and exit intent where traffic targeting was unfairly impacting results. In reality, when distributed to traffic properly 50/50, the two onsites performed similarly.

experiments.title: How a humanized slideup impacts donor conversion rate during a high-urgency match
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized slideup for users on CaringBridge profiles will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it humanizes the ask, and the letter-style sign-off presents the match opportunity from a person.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/03/2024
For CaringBridge’s Giving Tuesday match, we hypothesized that an iteration of the slide-up where the ask is presented by a person, with a letter-style sign-off would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that introducing a humanized slideup with a letter-style sign-off on CaringBridge profiles resulted in a 25.0% increase in non-tribute donations. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence is only 51.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for statistical significance.
Ultimately, this experiment was ended early and should be re-tested before these results can be used for decision making

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact callout on the tributes donation array impacts conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic impact array for users on the tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it encourages interaction with the array and clearly outlines the impact of a donor’s gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/03/2024
For CaringBridge’s Giving Tuesday match, we hypothesized that a dynamic impact array that automatically updated impact language based on the user’s gift selection would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
