The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamic impact array tested on the tribute donation page led to a 3.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 31.7%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is large enough to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider running the experiment for a longer duration to capture more diverse data and account for any external factors that may impact the outcome.
While the initial results of the dynamic impact array seem promising, it is important to validate these findings with a more rigorous experiment before implementing any changes based on this data. Further testing with a larger sample size and a longer duration will provide more reliable insights into the impact of the dynamic impact array on donor conversion rate and revenue.

experiments.title: How a Create a Post card with Ghost Text options impacts journal entries
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing host text options in the Create a Post card for authors will achieve an increase in new journal entries because it helps alleviate anxiety around what to post, while also eliminating friction by giving them a place to start.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/01/2024
In the next iteration of Create a Post testing, we hypothesized that the primary point of friction is that authors are not posting because they aren’t sure what to post on their page. We hypothesized that included a Create a Post card with a rotating option of Ghost Text to start their post would address these and increase journal entries. For context, Ghost Text options would be starting sentences like “A highlight to my week has been…” “Today, I’m grateful for…” “Something that’s been helping me lately is…”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Providing Ghost Text options in the Create a Post card for authors resulted in a 1.4% increase in New Journal Entries for All Traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on journal entries, the statistical level of confidence was only 44.0%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to think further about the types of prompts Authors are given, the callout Headline, and how the Create a Post card is shown with clear visibility. Additionally, we should also think about how the second opened card after they click aligns with messaging. Rather than "Create a Post" could it instead re-iterate the question, "How have you been?

experiments.title: How a slide out on a landing page impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide out with supportive copy for authors will achieve an increase in co-author invitations sent because it addresses the felt need and highlights it as a call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2024 - 10/24/2024
For CaringBridge authors, a key call to action is inviting a co-author to help manage your page. In order to increase this, we hypothesized that a slideout  with supportive copy would increase co-author invitations (email sign ups).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved a flyout with supportive copy for authors, resulted in an 8.9% increase in Co-Author Invites Sent for All Traffic. This indicates that the treatment had a positive impact on the desired outcome. However, the statistical level of confidence for this increase was 62.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.

It may be beneficial to further iterate on the flyout design and messaging to optimize the impact on Co-Author Invites Sent. Continue to monitor the engagement metrics such as Flyout CTA clicked to understand how users are interacting with the flyout.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is essential to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to validate the effectiveness of the flyout in increasing co-author invitations.

experiments.title: How an in-line prompt impacts new journal entries (non-clickable)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line Create a Post card for authors will achieve an increase in new journal entires because it prioritizes the CTA above others and makes it more visible..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2024 - 10/04/2024
For CaringBridge Authors, posting a new journal entry is one of the primary calls-to-action. However, currently it is given a similar level of priority as the other CTA’s on the left sidebar: Share, Add a Co-Author, Ways to Help, etc. We hypothesized that by placing this prompt in-line with the feed (where their entry would ultimately go) and it clickable would improve new journal entries — addressing a potential point of friction that authors aren’t sure where or how to post.
This was the first iteration in a testing series which presented an in-line card with a small clickable button – in the next test, the entire card was made clickable. Primarily, the impact of this change upon the first test was a significant decrease in clicks overall to the treatment, indicating that users did not know where to click or interact with the prompt.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While both experiments within this series resulted in a similar increase in journal entries (3%), the non-clickable card impacted this treatment negatively in clicks where users did not know what step to take next.

It is worth noting that making this clickable change didn't impact our end metric ultimately (journal entries) but it did improve the user experience.

experiments.title: How an in-line, clickable “Create a Post” button impacts new journal entries
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clickable, in-line “Create a Post” prompt for authors will achieve an increase in journal entries because it prioritizes it as a primary call-to-action by making it more visible for the author..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2024 - 09/24/2024
For CaringBridge Authors, posting a new journal entry is one of the primary calls-to-action. However, currently it is given a similar level of priority as the other CTA’s on the left sidebar: Share, Add a Co-Author, Ways to Help, etc. We hypothesized that by placing this prompt in-line with the feed (where their entry would ultimately go) and it clickable would improve new journal entries — addressing a potential point of friction that authors aren’t sure where or how to post.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The in-line "Create a Post" prompt led to a 3.3% increase in New Journal Entries for All Traffic. This indicates that making the journal entry prompt more visible and clickable can encourage authors to post more frequently.

2. However, the statistical confidence level of 79.6% is below the standard threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and not completely reliable for future decision making.

We believe that these soft results indicate that the true point of friction hasn't been addressed by this treatment. A similar level of clicks between the cards and a similar conversion rate of journal entires may indicate that "where" to post or "how" is not what's preventing authors from doing so. Rather, it's likely a question of "what" to post. How can we make it easier for authors to share what's on their mind, even if initially they may believe they don't have anything to say.

experiments.title: How adding an icon to the donation page showing the number of gifts made so far impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in gifts and revenue to the tribute widget because it makes this tab more visible, highlights its importance, and drives more users to the widget..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 09/13/2024
On CaringBridge profiles, the Tribute widget is a significant source of donations and revenue. A tribute donation is a gift to CaringBridge that a user makes in tribute to the profile they are visiting. This widget appears in a few different locations on the site, but historically one of the highest-performing locations has been on the Tribute page itself. This tab shows a list of tributes that have been made on the user’s profile, it includes copy “Join the X donors who have made a donation in honor of [name].
Since migration to CB2, CaringBridge has seen low performance on this widget. We hypothesized that by driving more traffic to the tab, it would increase donor conversion rate and revenue. On the old site, this Tributes tab was it’s own designated page and the button to navigate to it included a badge icon with how many tributes were on the profile. We created two treatments, one that’s a static badge icon and another where the same badge icon animates and counts up to the total number of tributes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab on site profiles significantly increased Revenue - Tribute from the widget by 106.7%. Additionally, Tribute Tab clicks experienced a substantial increase of 9,5881%. These results were statistically significant with confidence levels of 95.6% and 100% respectively.

Moving forward, it is clear that showcasing the importance of the Tribute tab by making it more visible and engaging users with a badge icon can lead to a significant boost in donations and revenue. This insight should be taken into consideration for future experimentation and site optimizations. By continuing to test and iterate on features that drive engagement with the Tribute widget, CaringBridge can further enhance user experience and ultimately increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a $25 callout on the Non-Tribute donation age impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $25 callout  for the non-tribute donation page will achieve an increase in revenue and donations because it reinforces the impact of the user’s gift in a tangible way — powering a user’s site..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 09/06/2024
For CaringBridge’s non-tribute donation page (main donate) we hypothesized that including a $25 callout over the gift array, leveraging language that’s been used successfully in other locations/widgets on the site, would improve overall revenue and transactions on main donate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a $25 callout for the non-tribute donation page resulted in a 3.0% increase in revenue for all traffic but with a statistical level of confidence of only 54%.
It is likely that this callout is not making a significant impact either way, because in both the control and treatment, the average gift is already above the $25 callout.
Moving forward, it is important to note that the results of this experiments hould not be be considered valid. It is essential to ensure that any future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the hypothesis and experiment design to potentially achieve more significant and reliable results. Testing different variations, such as adjusting the language or positioning of the callout, could also help to optimize future experiments.

experiments.title: How an educational tribute widget for new users impacts tribute gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an educational tribute section for new users on a profile without any tribute gifts will achieve an increase in tribute donations because it helps address confusion while also reinforcing what a tribute gift is and the impact it makes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2024 - 08/01/2024
When on a CaringBridge site, users can navigate to a profile’s “Tributes” tab that will catalog any tribute gifts made in honor of the profile. However, if a user does not have any Tributes gifts – this page would either be completely blank or present them with the normal Tribute Widget. Our hypothesis is that especially when thinking about new users, who may not be familiar with what a Tribute gift is, would be left confused on this tab. As a treatment, we developed an educational section that offered supporting copy on what the purpose of this tab was for, the impact of a tribute gift, and then presented the ask after this.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the introduction of an educational section for new users on a profile without any tribute gifts led to a 2.5% increase in Donations - Tribute for All Traffic. While this result shows a positive impact, the statistical level of confidence was only 35.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the educational section, test different messaging or designs, and consider targeting specific user segments to see if the impact varies.

Overall, the results of this experiment provide some indication that educating users about tribute gifts can potentially increase donations, but further testing is needed to confirm and build upon these findings.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Pop-Up on a Main Donation Page Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a monthly popup with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the popup reinforces the impact of a smaller amount than their original gift made on a consistent basis. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2024 - 01/12/2024
To increase monthly gifts on CaringBridge’s main donation page, we hypothesized a pop-up that presents the user with an opportunity to turn their original one-time amount into a lowered monthly gift would increase recurring donations. This pop-up would appear when the user submitted their one-time donation. We hypothesized that by making this conversion easy, suggesting a lower amount than what the user was originally going to donate, and reinforcing the impact of that smaller amount on a consistent basis it would improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which involved presenting a monthly pop-up with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation, resulted in a 25.0% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level of 77.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Our hypothesis for why this popup not only didn't increase monthly donations but in fact decreased donations overall is due to the way CaringBridge's form is preset. Monthly is already pre-selected as the default giving option, so to submit a one-time gift a user would have to intentionally click it, fill out the form, and press submit. Targeting that audience, who already has a specific motivation for making a one-time donation, with another popup likely made the experience more disruptive rather than presenting them with something they hadn't already considered.

In future testing, we will focus our efforts on that initial action — selecting one-time rather than monthly — at the top of the form so that we are meeting the donor at the point of consideration rather than at the end where they've already made a decision.

experiments.title: How a 2-Button CTA that Addresses Different Motivators Impacts Site Creations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a 2-button CTA for users on the CaringBridge homepage will achieve an increase in site creations because it better addresses both motivations for starting a site and ensures users who are creating a site for a loved one don’t feel excluded by the single CTA..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2023 - 12/29/2023
On CaringBridge’s homepage, a user has two distinct motivations for creating a site. Either for themself or for a loved one. We hypothesized that a single CTA “Create Site” didn’t properly address both audiences and believed that by speaking to both “Create a Site” and a second a button “Create a Site for a Loved One” would result in increased site conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The introduction of a second CTA button for creating a site for a loved one did not result in an increase in site creations. In fact, there was a decrease of 9.8% in New Site Created for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence for this decrease is 48.7%, which is below the threshold of 85% required for valid results.

Based on these learnings, it can be concluded that the addition of a second CTA button did not have the intended effect of increasing site creations.

In future testing, it would be beneficial to track clicks more closely and see how this impacts engagement even despite the relatively flat results in site creations.

experiments.title: How an animated lock on a donation page affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing security on the donation page for prospective tribute donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because there is a perceived increase in security and reduces anxiety for the prospective donor. because  there is a perceived increase in security and reduces anxiety for the prospective donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge already had an existing image of a security lock on their tribute donation pages.  We wanted to reinforce the perception of security even more by drawing attention to this part of the giving form. We made the lock an “animated” lock where it opened/closed every few seconds and we additional copy to remind the prospective donor that they are making a secure donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 4.9% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 71%.
Reinforcing the perception of security on the donation page can lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. An animated lock and additional copy reminding the prospective donor about the secure donation process can help reduce anxiety and increase donations. It's likely that because the security lock already existed on the page, security was already sufficiently highlighted for the end user.

experiments.title: How a slideout sticky donation prompt impacts giving on the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky donate prompt for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in giving because they are reminded to make a gift with accompanying value prop language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge had run a previous experiment on their resource pages that tested using a sticky donate button and prompting people to give when someone scrolls halfway through the page. We wanted to run the same test on the homepage to make sure it would not produce a negative impact on overall giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not result in a negative impact to giving - which was the goal of the experiment. The slideout resulted in a 3% increase in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 31% which is no impact in giving. When looking at the actual donations generated by this experiment we saw 48 gifts come in totaling in $2515 in revenue. These are gifts that would have likely not been captured without the experiment. It's recommended that this treatment is rolled out to the homepage.

experiments.title: How prompting people to give through a sticky donate button affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how CaringBridge has helped them by using value proposition language. because  they are being prompted to give and reminded how CaringBridge has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 06/19/2023
CaringBridge wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The first treatment would only slide out when people would click on it while the second treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version of the sticky donate button outperformed the control version, resulting in a 5.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85% (LoC 24%), indicating that the results may not be valid and should not be used for future decision making. The second treatment resulted in a 1% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 6%. Overall, it seems as if there is no impact but when seeing how many gifts were actually generated through each treatment, we saw 2 transactions generated by treatment 1 and 10 transactions generated by treatment 2. This shows us that treatment 2 is actually more successful in increase donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate (Pt2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prompting people to create a “page” or “journal” instead of a “site” will increase site creation rate because there will be a reduced perception of work involved which will reduce anxiety as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2023 - 06/26/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
When we ran the first iteration of this test we ran the experiment on one offer. We saw a valid lift when using “page.” Before rolling it out to all offers, we decided to test it among all offers and add another variant using “journal” to see if that would also reduce anxiety.
experiments.keyLearnings:
"Start a Page" resulted in a 27% decrease in site creation rate with a level of confidence of 75% while the "Start a Journal" treatment resulted in a 14% increase in site creation with a 40% statistical level of confidence, indicating a positive impact.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 85%.
Surprisingly, we did see a softness in response when we used "page." It's likely that some of the response is offer-specific but we did see a more positive response to using "journal." Which could indicate that journal lowers the barrier to entry for some reason.
It's worth to continue testing into this by reducing anxiety for the user by using supporting copy to highlight how easy it is to create a site.

experiments.title: Site vs Page on Caring for Cancer eBook Confirmation
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the call-to-action as Start a Page for users who’ve just signed up for an eBook offer will achieve an increase in site creations because creating a page has less perceived commitment than starting an entire site.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2023 - 05/21/2023
CaringBridge’s primary ask is to have users create a site — either for themselves or for a loved one. After signing up to be sent an offer, the user is taken to a confirmation page that prompts them with creating a site. We hypothesized that this phrasing could be overwhelming to a visitor who perhaps isn’t familiar with what CaringBridge is but was just interested in the eBook. We believe that the treatment, which instead updates the page with a call to Start a Page will lessen the perceived barrier to entry and increase the number of site creations. This same treatment was used on a different CaringBridge offer with great results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The changed call-to-action from "Start a Site" to "Start a Page" did not result in an increase in website creations. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in website creations. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.
Although this same treatment showed a huge increase in site creations on a different offer, the same did not result here. This might pose questions about how this offer and its audience may be different from the funnel where this treatment was last implemented. Ultimately, more testing is needed to create a valid result with a more significant sample size.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Do different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase downstream conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
Although there was no difference in clickthrough rate, treatment 3 (with the emotional appeal) produced a 67% increase in revenue through a combination of an increase in conversion rate and a simultaneous increase in average gift. This shows that while the initial metric was not affected, those who chose to click on the third treatment had a much higher motivation to give, which resulted in more gifts at larger sizes.
Treatment 3 offered something that none of the other treatments did—a way to show love and support for the journal author. While the other three treatments showed ways that the user could support the site and connected the site’s impact with the author, the third treatment gave them a direct connection to the person they care for, and proposed a donation as the way to show love and support. This produced an opportunity to give that no other treatment could match.
This experiment prompts more testing around this language throughout the site, as well as testing around how to increase clickthrough rate on the donate box through accentuated placement and visual UI.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.

The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They tested three different treatments to see if they could produce a lift in clickthrough rate:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
 	The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
 	The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
none of these experiments produced a significant lift in clickthrough rate

experiments.title: How simplifying the value proposition in a call to action impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying and focusing the value proposition language for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will add clarity to the call to action and reduce friction .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2023 - 04/11/2023
CaringBridge has a tribute widget on authors’ journal pages. This is an opportunity to make a donation as a tribute to keep the author’s site running. When looking at the original copy used in this tribute widget, we suspected that the value proposition used in the call to action might have competing motivations. It asked the person to be one X people who have already given and were given the suggested dollar amount to power the person’s site for an entire month. Plus, it asks if you can keep the site on for you and the author. Our hypothesis was that this call to action was actually adding friction to the decision making process. We wanted to simplify the call to action. Additionally we wanted to test if the removal of “CaringBridge” in the CTA would make the ask less organizational centric and clearer. The second treatment also focuses on just making the gift in honor of the person.
Treatment 1: A $30 donation to CaringBridge powers a site like Jan’s for one month. Will you join the X donors who have made a donation in honor of Laura with your donation today?
Treatment 2: X donors have made a donation in honor of Laura. Will you join them with your donation today?
The first treatment simplifies the ask so that it does not ask the person to keep the site online for them and for you. The second treatment simplifies the CTA even further by removing mention of CaringBridge and how much is needed to power a site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in no conclusive "winner" when looking at donor conversion rate. Treatment 1 resulted in a 2.8% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 31% while treatment 3 resulted in a 3.4% increase in donations with a 38.4% statistical level of confidence.
These treatments resulted in no change in conversion rate but there does seem to be a valid increase in revenue. Treatment 1 produced a 33% valid lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 98%. Treatment 2 produced a 19.5% lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 92%.
This implies that while the language doesn't inspire more people to give, it does motivate people to give even larger gifts. It's likely that the first treatment provided a larger lift in revenue than treatment 2 because it kept the specific $30 ask in the copy.

experiments.title: How addressing the felt need of the ebook recipient impacts account creations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language that addressed the felt need for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in site creation rate because the “why” of a site creation would be established before being asked to create one, thus reducing friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 04/09/2023
In oder to increase the conversion rate for site creations, we ran a test where we added language that addressed the felt need of the ebook recipient.
This test was ran on an ebook funnel where the confirmation page for their ebook had a call to action to create a site with CaringBridge. The control for this experiment invited the individual to “start a CaringBridge site” and was an explicit as to why someone would need a CaringBridge site. We believed that changing the language to address the felt need would decrease friction the person would experience in being asked to create a site right away.
This was tested on one ebook funnel and traffic was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version of the call to action saw a 7.6% increase in website creations for all traffic with a 21.0% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making. The results indicate that changing the language to address the felt need had little to no impact on the site creation rate. It is likely that the change in copy was not significant enough to sway the recipient to take action.
For future experiments, it is recommended to continue testing language that addresses the felt need of the audience for increased website creations. Perhaps a complete rewrite may be more impactful.

experiments.title: How a premium affects recurring conversion rate on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it provides an incentive to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2023 - 02/02/2023
CaringBridge saw strategic value in growing their recurring donor program, and wanted to run an experiment on their main donation page. Through a partnership with Bicycle cards, they had the chance to offer a unique deck of playing cards in exchange for a recurring gift.
They wanted to test this concept to see if it would increase recurring gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 27 days and the results indicate that overall conversion saw a significant increase of 3.4% when adding the premium incentive. However, this result is too small to be considered statistically valid due to a 5.9% level of confidence.
Overall, the key learning from this experiment suggests that recurring donors do not respond positively when provided with incentives. Future experiments should focus on better understanding the motivation as to why people want to give recurring gifts, attempting to tailor incentives in a way that resonates most with their movement. This, in turn, will lead to sustainable increases in conversions and eventually, customer loyalty.

experiments.title: How anniversary language and monthly donor language affect conversion rate on a monthly giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding anniversary language and changing “partner” to “monthly donor” for returning visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
CaringBridge had used popups to ask repeat visitors to consider giving a monthly gift. While the performance had built a file of recurring donors, they wanted to see if they could increase the clickthrough rate and conversion rate to monthly donor. They formed two hypotheses:
Using language highlighting their 25th anniversary would increase relevance and clickthrough rate and conversion rate
Using “monthly donor” rather than “CaringBridge partner” would increase the clarity of the ask and increase conversion rate.
They decided to test this with an A/B/C test. Their first treatment introduced the 25th anniversary language, but kept the “partner” language. The second kept the 25th anniversary language, but introduced “monthly donor” in place of “partner”.
They launched this to determine a winning variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there weren't enough monthly transactions to validate the experiment, the second treatment (which included both the added relevance of the 25th anniversary language and the clarity of the term "monthly donor") showed a non-valid lift of 42%.
The low conversion rate made this unlikely to validate, as the difference was 0.00008205212361% for the control and 0.0001166588894 for the second treatment. However, it was clear that the first treatment underperformed, so they decided to run this experiment with only two variants to see if they could deliver a valid result.

experiments.title: How “hero-focused” language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging hero language for previous donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/22/2022
Donors are heroes. Or, so the common logic goes. CaringBridge had heard over and over again at conferences and speaking engagements how important it is to make the donor feel like a hero. So, they decided to test it themselves.
In their first appeal for their anniversary campaign, they had a very personal story from the campaign manager. They decided to replace this copy with copy that highlighted the donor as the hero.
They tested this by splitting their email file in half and sending one treatment to each half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results weren't statistically valid, there was a directional lift using the "hero" copy that focused the attention on the donors.

This will need to be re-tested, but signs point to the fact that giving the donor a sort of platitude that makes them feel good can increase results.

experiments.title: How “hero-focused” language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging hero language for previous donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/22/2022
Donors are heroes. Or, so the common logic goes. CaringBridge had heard over and over again at conferences and speaking engagements how important it is to make the donor feel like a hero. So, they decided to test it themselves.
In their first appeal for their anniversary campaign, they had a very personal story from the campaign manager. They decided to replace this copy with copy that highlighted the donor as the hero.
They tested this by splitting their email file in half and sending one treatment to each half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results weren't statistically valid, there was a directional lift using the "hero" copy that focused the attention on the donors.

This will need to be re-tested, but signs point to the fact that giving the donor a sort of platitude that makes them feel good can increase results.

experiments.title: How giving the donor autonomy during a match affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving the donor autonomy for prospective donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2022
As CaringBridge was preparing to launch their holiday appeal, they wanted to test integrating the concept of autonomy in how they asked donors to give when their gift was matched. Traditionally, they would ask the donors to give and tell them their gift would be matched. But they decided to add a second layer to this by asking the donor to “allow the Board to match their gift”.
The hypothesis is that giving the donor more autonomy—or power in the transaction—would increase giving.
They split their email file to test the concept.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a slight increase in conversion rate, but not to a statistically valid level.
In the control, the offer to the donor is to "get their gift matched". But autonomy puts more power in the donor's hands—so the treatment copy gave the donor the POWER to allow the Board to match their gift. In essence, it placed the Board in a position of servitude to the donor, rather than making them the most important (and generous) part of the transaction.
This will be re-tested in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How continuity-based messaging affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing continuity-based messaging for people interested in becoming a recurring donor will achieve increased donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 12/10/2020
CaringBridge had seen experiments where adding a banner with the value proposition “Support 365 days a year” had increased recurring gift conversion on donation pages. This type of language helped the donor understand the continuity of their impact, and helped make the value proposition for a monthly recurring donation by reminding the donor that their impact went far beyond that initial gift.
Since CaringBridge had a recurring-specific funnel triggered by a popup for engaged visitors, they decided to launch this experiment. They added a banner at the top of the monthly page to express this “continuity-based” message to the visitor, and split the traffic to see how it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the banner had a directional increase of 16% in converting page visitors to recurring donors. However, this sample size was too small due to the overall low conversion rate, and it did not reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How continuity-based messaging affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing continuity-based messaging for people interested in becoming a recurring donor will achieve increased donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 12/10/2020
CaringBridge had seen experiments where adding a banner with the value proposition “Support 365 days a year” had increased recurring gift conversion on donation pages. This type of language helped the donor understand the continuity of their impact, and helped make the value proposition for a monthly recurring donation by reminding the donor that their impact went far beyond that initial gift.
Since CaringBridge had a recurring-specific funnel triggered by a popup for engaged visitors, they decided to launch this experiment. They added a banner at the top of the monthly page to express this “continuity-based” message to the visitor, and split the traffic to see how it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the banner had a directional increase of 16% in converting page visitors to recurring donors. However, this sample size was too small due to the overall low conversion rate, and it did not reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How communal language on a primary donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that applying communal language for main donation page visitors  will achieve increase conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 12/20/2020
CaringBridge had tested “communal” copy that reduced transactional language and attempted to build a relationship with the user first to great success on a series of popups throughout their site.
They wanted to test this copy on their main donation page during the holiday season. They changed the question-based headline to a complimentary headline thanking the donor for their consideration, and put the power to give in the user’s hands with “if it’s not too much to ask”. They also used language that had worked on the popup that removed the Board of Directors from a position of power and put them in a position of service.
experiments.keyLearnings: All in all, the communal language failed to have much of an impact at all in the time that it ran. Motivation is very high for this page, so it can be difficult to affect conversion rate. On further observation, the headline may be confusing for visitors, and that might need to be reworked.

experiments.title: How the order of a signup flow affects conversion on desktop devices
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reversing the order of login options for mobile visitors will achieve an increase in user signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2021 - 04/03/2021
CaringBridge had two options to create an account: using your email, or using single-sign-on (SSO) options like “Login with Google”, “Login with AppleID”, or “Login with Facebook”. Traditionally, these had been vertically ordered email-first, then SSO options.
The team wondered if the rising popularity of SSO options (primarily for reasons of security) would imply that these options should be listed first. They created a simple test to flip the options to see if the SSO-first approach would result in more signups and created CaringBridge sites.
The desktop version of this test showed a decrease in signups—however, the mobile showed promise. So they decided to isolate the mobile test to keep it running longer and cut their losses on desktop signups.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, this experiment failed to produce a winning treatment with any difference. While there was a small directional lift, it was not close to statistical validity. The team moved on to higher priority tests to drive more new users.

experiments.title: How emotional impact language on the tribute widget affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding emotional impact language for personal home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2021 - 05/04/2021
CaringBridge’s tribute widget was a primary source of donations for people who visited their journals. Over the years, they had optimized the copy to maximize conversion.
However, as they reviewed the latest control, they wondered if they could better convey the emotional impact that a journal provides for someone who is going through a health journey, and if that would translate into more gifts or more revenue.
They created a treatment that made two tweaks to the copy. First, they changed the headline to include the impact, as well as the result. Instead of just “Help [FirstName] Stay Connected To Family and Friends”, they added the effect: “Help [FirstName] Experience the Comfort of Staying Connected To Family and Friends”. Then, in the body copy, they inserted language that referred to the potential feeling of loss the author might feel without Caringbridge: “A $30 donation to CaringBridge means that [FirstName] won’t lose this special connection to family and friends.
Then, they split traffic to see the impact of this new emotional impact language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, there was no impact on conversion rate. This suggests a few potential reasons why:
The reader might have already understood the emotional impact, and the specific callout only brought that to the surface—it wasn't missing. Any impact the emotional language might have had could have been blunted by the additional copy. The copy was ultimately not that different from the original.
Either way, this experiment failed to move the needle, and more radical approaches must be taken to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How prioritizing personalization on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing the first name of the connection for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2021 - 06/04/2021
CaringBridge’s tribute donation page is where the majority of their conversions happen. As people are reading journal pages, they are able to give to support the site of their friend.
This page had long had a “cause and effect” value proposition that had not been challenged in some time. In reviewing this copy, they noticed that the organization name came before the “familiar” name of the friend whose journal they were reading.
The team came up with two treatments—one that just reversed the order of the “familiar” and organization names, and then another that also made a more direct ask and included language about a “vital connecting point” to increase the strength of the case for support.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither treatment moved the needle. Both treatments had slight decreases in conversion rate that weren't going to validate, and slight increases in revenue (through larger average gifts) that weren't going to validate either.
With a 34-35% donor conversion rate, it might prove more fruitful to get more motivated traffic from upstream to funnel into this page.

experiments.title: How relevant, non-specific “crisis” language affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will relevant, non-specific “crisis” language affects conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2020 - 04/01/2020
When COVID-19 became a worldwide pandemic, the importance of CaringBridge became greater. As people were sheltering-in-place at their homes, the digital connections CaringBridge helped facilitate were more significant—which also increased the urgency to support CaringBridge as a platform. They wanted to find the right way to integrate this into their fundraising message, hoping that it would increase conversion rate. They created messaging that didn’t address COVID-19 specifically, as not to be exploitative, but addressed the larger sentiment. Then, they tested this in their highest revenue placement, the tribute widget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After seeing an initial increase in conversion (that got as high as 15% in the first week), the data normalized at no significant increase in response. This could be because the copy was NOT specific to COVID-19, but could also reflect the fact that prospective donors did not share the sentiment that CaringBridge becomes more valuable in crisis times. There are two possible paths to take here:
Continue to test non-time-sensitive copy on the tribute widgetBe more explicit in the COVID-19 messaging to see if response can be increased with more specificity.

experiments.title: How using a brand name in donor acquisition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will using a brand name in donor acquisition affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2019 - 08/04/2019
CaringBridge had long acquired many donors through their “tribute giving“ pages, which allow people to make a gift in honor of a friend or loved one using CaringBridge. The language on the donation page let the donor know that their gift made “this site” possible. The CaringBridge customer care team hypothesized that using the name “CaringBridge” instead of “this site” would increase clarity and decrease refund rate. The development team was open to this, but wanted to make sure that using the brand name didn’t negatively affect conversion. They developed a page with the same creative, but using “CaringBridge” in place of “this site”.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the brand name performed no differently than the “this site” language. This was a positive development as it cleared the way to test this downstream to increase clarity and reduce refund rate.

experiments.title: How a dropdown on a donate button affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a drop down navigation option for one-time and recurring gifts increase overall (or recurring) giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2019 - 07/20/2019
For Caringbridge, the primary navigation has always had a prominent donation button in the top right corner. This button took all potential donors to a generic donation page that offered both one-time and recurring giving options.
There was a hypothesis that since a recurring donor and a one-time donor are two distinct segments, it may make sense to split this audience at the navigation level. Doing this would allow for targeted donation pages for each segment which may help increase the relevance and perceived value of the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months with over 10 million visitors, we found that the drop down navigation offer had no statistically significant impact on overall giving or the likelihood for individuals to give a recurring gift. This could potentially suggest that we're bringing this split too high up into the conversion funnel. Instead of the navigation, it may make more sense to emphasize this segmentation split (with associated value proposition) on the donation page itself. Similar experiments have been run using tabs on donation forms with success. That would be a natural next step.

experiments.title: How “impact language” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “impact language” that describes the outcome of the gift increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2019 - 08/09/2019
CaringBridge’s main donation page had always been difficult to test, since it had a relatively low volume of traffic and a relatively high conversion rate. However, they didn’t want settle for “good enough”, so they kept testing. The control copy asked “What does CaringBridge mean to you” and had a highly personal appeal that proposed various emotions that might be brought to the surface when one uses CaringBridge. They wanted to test “impact language” that talked about what CaringBridge does instead of what it means to the individual viewer. They drafted new copy that very simply told the viewer what would happen when they gave—loved ones would be connected.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the treatment failed to produce any lift, most likely due to the high motivation of donors, which makes it difficult to increase motivation through copy. The next test on this page will be a recurring donation ask popup that seeks to increase lifetime donor value rather than conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a higher gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a gift array with higher values affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2019 - 03/14/2019
CaringBridge had used a giving infrastructure that had separate mobile and desktop donation pages. As a result, certain optimizations had not been rolled out to both experiences, resulting in slight differences between the two pages. In previous tests, CaringBridge had “unified” the copy on the page by testing out of all negative impact.
However, the mobile version still had another gift array that started at $25, unlike the others that started at $50. As their final “unification” test, CaringBridge wanted to test the $50 array that was found everywhere else to make sure there was no impact on revenue.
The control had an array of $25, $50, $100, $250. They launched a treatment with the array found on the other pages: $50, $100, $250, Other.
experiments.keyLearnings: After more than 700 transactions, the new gift array had no significant difference in conversions, which gave CaringBridge the all-clear to launch the unified experience. This process, though it took time, is the proof-positive way to launch new experiences. CaringBridge could have easily just launched the same copy and donation array everywhere, but there could have been serious ramifications in revenue. This series of unification tests shows that there was no risk at all.

experiments.title: How a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2019
CaringBridge’s donation process had always had a separate page for mobile devices because of some legacy technology. Instead of loading a responsive page, the CaringBridge site used redirects to send mobile users to a separate page entirely. Over time, this page had not kept up with all the optimizations that had been implemented on the desktop page. CaringBridge decided to test the two pages against each other to see the cost and benefit of rolling both pages into one responsive page, which would keep them from having to keep two pages updated and in sync.
The first major difference in the two pages was the gift array. This was tested independently, and had no impact on conversion or revenue. The next major change was the value proposition and layout of the form. The mobile page had always had a shorter value proposition because it was assumed that they had less “real estate” on the page to hold such copy. The team decided to test a mobile version of the exact same donation page that desktop users saw to see the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no change—and even a slight directional increase with the new copy. This gave CaringBridge the confidence to revert to a single, mobile-optimized donation page for their tribute funnel. Going forward, they will monitor desktop and mobile results independently to see how future experiments affect conversion on each.

experiments.title: How personalization and value proposition affect recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does personalization (and the presence of value proposition copy in the first place) affect conversion for recurring donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2018
CaringBridge had recently reintroduced the recurring gift option in their tribute page donation process. While a previous test had showed that a single checkbox with value proposition copy beat a binary (monthly/one-time) option, they wanted to test the impact of personalizing the copy to the subject of the journal. While they were doing that, they wanted to also test a version that had no additional value proposition copy to establish a baseline conversion rate, since their first test was a radical redesign. They designed two treatments—one with personalized copy and one with just “Make this a monthly recurring donation” and launched an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there weren't enough conversions to validate any of the treatments, the final treatment with no value proposition copy showed a directional 40% decrease in conversion, with a growing level of confidence. This implies (though not statistically valid) that any amount of value proposition copy is better than no value proposition copy. There was no difference at all in conversion by adding personalization (which goes against the grain of previous testing), but also shows that we must work harder at this ask to increase results, as it is a secondary value proposition—asking the donor to "lift" a little more (with a monthly donation) when they are already making a lift as it is (with a single donation).

experiments.title: How “minor mystery” language in a headline affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will “minor mystery” language in a headline affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2018 - 01/14/2018
CaringBridge had run extensive testing on their “tribute widget”, where the majority of donations on their site begin. They wanted to run a headline test, with the knowledge that such a slight change might not produce any noticeable effect. They wanted to test the angle of a “minor mystery”, which had proven in other experiments to increase engagement (and subsequently conversion) by provoking the reader that they might not know something.
The headline on the tribute widget asked a simple question: Can you help power [tribute name]’s site? They proposed a test to the headline to introduce the “minor mystery”: Did you know you can help power [tribute name]’s site?
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no significant difference in the two headlines. This means that the next experiment needs to be more radical in nature to affect results.

experiments.title: How placement of a call-to-action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the placement of the call-to-action button increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2016 - 03/30/2016
CaringBridge developed an email appeal for their March 2016 fundraising campaign. They had traditionally put the call-to-action buttons in the body of the email, giving the donor a large clickable area to click through and make their donation. However, since their email had a postscript, they hypothesized that putting the call-to-action at the end of the email might make more sense, as it allowed the user to digest all the copy before taking action.
They split their email file 50/50 to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment did not produce a significant lift. This shows us that the placement of the button does not have a significant effect on the clickthrough rate. Subsequent tests showed that removing the button actually improved results, so this element does not appear in current emails.

experiments.title: How prioritizing match language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does putting match language before a story affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2016 - 01/17/2017
CaringBridge had a matching gift offered during their year-end campaign from a user who had personally experienced the value proposition of the organization. They knew that stories had been powerful vehicles for fundraising campaigns, but this story had the added power of a matching gift.
They deliberated whether to put the match announcement at the beginning of the email or after the story. Putting it at the beginning would allow people who were already motivated to give to make their donations right away. But putting it at the end might allow people to be drawn in by the story first and potentially result in more donations once the powerful story was augmented by the match. They decided to put it to a test and split their file to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which led with the matching gift announcement, produced a 32.7% lift in conversion, although the experiment did not reach statistical validity. This suggests that the directness of the match language was a powerful motivator, or that it possibly framed the story and resulted in more donors being led to take action.
However, this test will need to receive more traffic to produce a valid result.

experiments.title: How a signature in an email affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding a signature image to an email affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2016 - 01/17/2017
CaringBridge was sending a personal email from a development team member as a follow-up to a matching gift announcement. They wondered about the inclusion of an actual signature in an email. People don’t normally sign emails, but many organizations brought this tactic over from direct mail appeals.
Typically, their emails did not include signatures, but they created a treatment to see if this small change added validity to the sender or decreased the personal feel of the email.
They split their email file to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Adding the signature produced a directional 32.9% lift in donor conversion—which meant that despite it being a non-native email element, it appears to have positively affected conversion. However, at only 72.9% statistical confidence, it needs to be tested with a larger audience to reach validity.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.
The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They wanted to test different messages to see if they could radically increase clickthrough rate, and with that increase in traffic, increase conversion rate and revenue.
They developed three treatments:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
Since CaringBridge has large amounts of traffic, they launched a 4-way test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: After several weeks of testing, none of the copy variants produced any significant lift over the control. While this was disappointing, a further review of the data found that one of the variants produced a significant increase in revenue after the click. This showed that the value propositions might not produce a result immediately, but might inspire a higher motivation to give.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security on a donate button affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the donate button inside the secure box increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 08/15/2017
CaringBridge had performed extensive testing on their “tribute” donation form, which produced the majority of their online fundraising revenue. One experiment was able to increase the conversion rate on the page by increasing perceived security, adding a box around the credit card information. However, this box left the donate button outside of the box. Their team wondered if this was leaving conversions on the table due to the button being perceived as “insecure”.
They launched a version of the page that moved the donate button into the “secure box” and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test failed to produce a valid result in either direction. While the secure box produced a lift, simply moving the donate button inside the box was not enough of a difference-maker to affect conversion positively or negatively.

experiments.title: How direct copy affects donation page conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does more direct copy increase the force of the value proposition and result in more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2017 - 08/03/2017
CaringBridge’s primary donation page had proven to show better results with a broader value proposition, as opposed to the highly personalized value proposition presented on their tribute donation pages.
In a copy review of their tribute donation page, they wondered if the question posed in the headline: “What does CaringBridge mean to you?” added cognitive friction as people tried to answer this question for themselves, instead of continuing to ingest the value proposition.
They created a treatment that instantly offered an opportunity to the reader—”You make CaringBridge better.” The treatment copy removed questions and made statements, telling them that if they valued CaringBridge, their donation was vital.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment did not produce a statistically significant result either way, which showed that more radical changes need to be made to the page to deliver a result. Since this page has a high donor conversion rate and low traffic, it might be difficult to prove a winning treatment due to higher visitor motivation…but we will continue testing.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array on mobile devices affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does an open field on a donation form affect donor conversion rate on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2017 - 06/15/2017
CaringBridge had seen a significant lift in donor conversion on one of their donation pages when they removed the gift array and simply offered an “open field” that allowed donors to pick their own donation amount. However, their donation page setup split out desktop pages and mobile pages, so they wanted to test the exact same premise on their mobile pages.
They removed the array on a treatment page and left an open field, then launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though the experiment didn’t validate, CaringBridge saw quite the opposite result on mobile devices—a 10.5% decrease in conversion when the open field was presented. This could be due to the fact that the buttons become larger on mobile devices and are much easier to use on a small screen. It could also be related to the situational difference between desktop (likely seated at a desk) and mobile (out and about, with many more possible distractions).

experiments.title: How emotion-driven copy affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotive copy increase donation revenue on a tribute donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2017 - 08/31/2017
CaringBridge had previously run an experiment on the journal pages of their site that produced a big lift  in revenue when “emotive” language was shown. They wanted to run an additional test to see if the same sort of emotive language would produce an increase in revenue on the tribute donation page. Since the the winning copy from the previous experiment wouldn’t deploy for a few weeks, they had a chance to test the copy on the tribute page independently. They hypothesized that if they could also get a lift on the donation page, then they might be able to have a compound lift from the two areas of improvement.
They created a treatment donation page that utilized the “love and support” language and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page failed to produce any significant difference from the control donation page. This shows that while the emotive language might be a great motivator to start the gift process, it doesn’t have a significant effect at finishing the donation process. It also may show a relationship between the emotive language in the donate box and the standard control language on the donation page—these two copy variations may be working in tandem to produce a higher revenue total.
